Colombia_Justification:
The National Action Plan (NAP) on Mercury in Artisanal and Small-Scale Gold Mining in Colombia (2024) integrates intersectional approaches into its diagnosis, strategies, and actions. The document recognizes the different forms of vulnerability associated with mercury exposure, taking into account the dimensions of gender, age, ethnicity, and territory.

In its situational analysis, the NAP identifies the differentiated impacts of mercury on women, Indigenous communities, Afro-Colombian communities, and rural populations. It notes that women involved in artisanal and small-scale gold mining (ASGM)—particularly in amalgamation and processing activities—face greater risks due to direct exposure and their role in family health care.

Likewise, the plan promotes a gender and social inclusion approach, incorporated across all action strategies. Strategy 3 (“Reduction of Emissions, Releases, and Exposure Risks”) establishes the need to design awareness and environmental education programs with a differential approach, targeting vulnerable communities. Strategy 4 (“Capacity building and knowledge generation”) proposes training women miners in mercury-free practices and community leadership.

Question: Are there government-led monitoring systems for environmental and social impacts of ASM?
Bolivia_Justification:
Current environmental regulations, specifically Supreme Decree No. 3549, stipulate that the representative of the Mining and Metallurgical Activities (AOP) must conduct ongoing environmental monitoring to ensure compliance with the commitments made in the Environmental License (Art. 6). The Environmental Prevention and Control Regulations establish environmental monitoring as an instrument of Environmental Quality Control (Art. 98).

The Air Pollution Regulations, as well as the Water Pollution Regulations, in Articles 68 and 74 respectively, establish that monitoring must be carried out semi-annually for the first five years after the Environmental License is issued, for those AOPs that began operations prior to the enactment of these regulations.

While the Environmental and Metallurgical Activities Regulations (RAAM) establish a monitoring system, this pertains to the accumulation of mining and metallurgical waste, with the aim of periodically assessing the stability of the infrastructure and the effectiveness of the pollution control and prevention system (Arts. 42 to 44).

- D.S. Nº 3549. Disponible en: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/modulos/Biblioteca/items/03092021-010949-532781_209/03092021-010949-532781_209.pdf
- Reglamento de Prevención y Control Ambiental. Disponible en: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/modulos/Biblioteca/items/03092021-010949-532781_209/03092021-010949-532781_209.pdf
- Reglamento en Materia de Contaminación Atmosférica. Disponible en: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/modulos/Biblioteca/items/03092021-010949-532781_209/03092021-010949-532781_209.pdf
- Regalmento en Materia de Contaminación Hídrica. Disponible en: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/modulos/Biblioteca/items/03092021-010949-532781_209/03092021-010949-532781_209.pdf
- Reglamento Ambiental para Actividades Mineras. Disponible en: https://sea.gob.bo/digesto/CompendioII/N/149_DS_24782.pdf
Brazil_Justification:
Brazil has comprehensive, government-led monitoring systems to assess the environmental and social impacts of artisanal and small-scale mining (ASSM). These systems are in place and actively used to inform policies, enforce regulations, and mitigate the negative impacts of mining.

A recent study by the Ministry of Mines and Energy (MME) provides a detailed picture of gold mining in Brazil and proposes pathways to legal and sustainable activity. In July 2025, the MME delivered the Panorama of Small-Scale Mining, a groundbreaking study on gold mining in Brazil. Coordinated by the MME, the preparation of the diagnosis was supported by the United Nations through the Global Environment Facility. The execution was carried out by the Foundation for Technological Development in Engineering (FDTE) and the Research Center for Responsible Small-Scale Mining (Nap.Mineração) of USP.

The study highlights the economic importance of small-scale gold mining (SM&M), which accounts for 27% of national gold production, but also acknowledges the serious associated environmental and social problems, such as deforestation and mercury use. The Ministry of Mines and Energy (MME), in collaboration with the Intergovernmental Forum on Mining (IGF), created a SM&M task force to lead the formalization and promote sustainability. This task force is developing regulations for health, safety, and environmental protection, as well as licensing and certification regimes.

Furthermore, the Federal Public Prosecutor's Office (MPF) and the Federal Police are using the "Gold Mining Impact Calculator," a tool developed in partnership with the Conservation Strategy Fund (CSF) and the World Bank, to economically assess the social and environmental damages of small-scale gold mining. This tool has been instrumental in increasing fines for environmental damage, especially those related to mercury pollution, by almost tenfold.

Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has government-led environmental monitoring systems, but these are not specifically designed to assess the impacts of artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM). The Institute of Hydrology, Meteorology and Environmental Studies (IDEAM) manages the Forest and Carbon Monitoring System (SMByC), which monitors deforestation and can detect mining activities, but does not specifically assess the social impacts of ASM [5].
The Regional Autonomous Corporations (CARs) monitor environmental licenses, but generally for all mining projects, not specifically for ASM.

Peru_Justification: On the one hand, the OEFA operates the Interactive Environmental Enforcement Portal (PIFA), which includes maps, information on entities subject to enforcement, monitoring of inspections, and statistics on enforcement actions in various sectors, including mining. It also publishes reports and specific actions related to the enforcement of regulations concerning small-scale and artisanal mining. However, these are more effective in large-scale and medium-scale mining. The Ministry of the Environment (MINAM) has the National Environmental Information System (SINIA), which centralizes environmental information (indicators, maps, statistics, publications, and technical guides) that can be used to assess environmental impacts related to artisanal and small-scale mining (land cover, water quality, mercury contamination, etc.). The Ministry of Energy and Mines (MINEM) is promoting the Interoperable System for Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining, a digital tool that will allow for real-time monitoring of the activities carried out by small producers and artisanal miners and aims to facilitate the exchange of information among all entities involved in mining formalization. Despite this, monitoring in artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) is currently weak due to informality and a lack of resources. No evidence has been found of government-led social monitoring systems specifically for ASM.

Question: Is satellite or geospatial monitoring used to detect illegal or unlicensed ASM activities?
Bolivia_Justification: AJAM, for its part, in meetings with the PlanetGOLD Bolivia project, has expressed its interest in obtaining permits to access high-resolution satellite images that would allow them to identify illegal mining through satellite systems.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Reuniones asistidas.
- Informes de Amazon Conservation y MAAP
- Conferencias de SERGEOMIN
Brazil_Justification:
Brazil actively employs satellite and geospatial monitoring to detect illegal or unlicensed artisanal and small-scale gold mining (ASM) activities. The National Institute for Space Research (INPE) uses remote sensing, including the DETER system, to monitor deforestation and mining in protected areas. For example, the DETER system identified an increase in illegal mining on Yanomami lands in 2021, leading to government action.

Furthermore, advanced technologies are being developed and utilized. A new machine learning algorithm analyzes Sentinel-2 satellite imagery to quickly and accurately detect new deforestation fronts for gold mining with a resolution of 10 meters. This algorithm revealed that deforestation for gold mining in the Yanomami Indigenous Territory was much greater than previously thought, with over 2,000 hectares deforested since 2019, and 67% of that occurring in 2022.

Non-governmental organizations also contribute to this monitoring. Greenpeace Brazil developed the 'Papa Alpha' georeferencing tool, which processes satellite data in near real-time to monitor deforestation and illegal mining. Papa Alpha integrates data from two systems: GLAD (Global Land Analysis and Discovery), which uses optical satellite imagery to detect changes every 5 days with 30x30 meter alerts, and RADD (Radar for Detecting Deforestation), which uses Sentinel-1 radar images to penetrate clouds for more precise and near real-time detection of forest disturbances.

These systems indicate that monitoring is carried out with detection capabilities at least annually and, in some cases, much more frequently (e.g., every 5 days or near real-time).
Brazil_Sources:
https://envolverde.com.br/politica-publica/ambiente/como-as-imagens-de-satelite-combatem-a-mineracao-ilegal-na-amazonia-brasileira/
https://www.maapprogram.org/pt-br/maap-181-portugues/
https://www.greenpeace.org/brasil/blog/conheca-o-papa-alpha-o-sistema-de-monitoramento-do-greenpeace-brasil/
Colombia_Justification:
Yes. Colombia continuously uses satellite and geospatial monitoring systems to detect and track illegal or unauthorized mining activities, including those related to artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM).

The Institute of Hydrology, Meteorology and Environmental Studies (IDEAM) operates the Forest and Carbon Monitoring System (SMByC), which generates quarterly and annual deforestation alerts by analyzing medium- and high-resolution satellite imagery (Landsat, Sentinel, Planet). These alerts allow for the identification of forest cover loss patterns associated with illegal mining, especially in the Amazon region. The results are shared with the Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development, the Attorney General's Office, and the Armed Forces, supporting environmental and judicial enforcement operations.

The Colombian Mining Information System (SIMCO), managed by the Ministry of Mines and Energy and the National Mining Agency (ANM), integrates georeferenced information on mining titles, pending applications, and excluded or restricted areas. This database allows for cross-referencing information with satellite imagery to detect unlicensed activities or activities outside authorized areas.

Decree 1993 of 2002 establishes SIMCO as the official mining information system, mandating the continuous updating of the sector's maps and geospatial records. In practice, this data is used by the Organized Crime Command of the Ministry of National Defense in surveillance operations such as Operation Artemis, which focuses on the satellite detection and dismantling of illegal mining operations.

Additionally, the Alluvial Gold Mining Report (EVOA), prepared by the United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC) under a cooperation agreement with the Government of Colombia, is a fundamental source of geospatial monitoring. This report is developed in coordination with the Ministry of Mines and Energy and the Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development, and uses satellite imagery and geospatial analysis to identify and classify alluvial gold mining areas throughout the country.
Colombia_Sources:
IDEAM – Sistema de Monitoreo de Bosques y Carbono (SMByC). Disponible en: https://www.ideam.gov.co/nuestra-entidad/ecosistemas-e-informacion-ambiental/sistema-monitoreo-bosques-carbono

SIMCO – Sistema de Información Minero Colombiano. Disponible en: https://www.simco.gov.co/

Decreto 1993 de 2002 (por el cual se crea el SIMCO como sistema oficial de información minera). Disponible en: https://minenergia.gov.co/es/repositorio-normativo/normativa/sistema-de-informaci%C3%B3n-minera/

UNODC – Informe de Explotación de Oro de Aluvión (EVOA), en coordinación con el Gobierno de Colombia. Disponible en: https://www.datos.gov.co/Minas-y-Energ-a/Explotaci-n-de-Oro-de-Aluvi-n-EVOA-por-Departament/ub8r-2956/about_data
Peru_Justification: Yes, it uses satellite and geospatial monitoring to detect unlicensed or illegal mining activities (including artisanal and small-scale mining). It does so continuously/near real-time through satellite alerts and then aggregates/analyzes that data into products on a monthly, quarterly, or annual basis, depending on the platform and report. MINAM, through SINIA and the Forest Program, uses Landsat and Sentinel imagery to generate early deforestation alerts (frequent visual verifications), enabling continuous detection of changes in forest cover linked to mining. OEFA, through its Interactive Environmental Enforcement Portal, publishes real-time monitoring maps and data and uses geospatial tools to support environmental enforcement. MINEM is implementing SIPMMA (Interoperable System for Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining), which will integrate administrative records with satellite imagery and GPS tracking for monitoring and traceability. On the other hand, through international cooperation, Peru collaborates with initiatives such as SERVIR / NASA and with independent platforms (RAMI, MAAP, ACCA) that provide real-time detection of mining and deforestation hotspots.

Question: Can governmental satellite monitoring systems distinguish between artisanal, semi-mechanized, and mechanized mining activities?
Bolivia_Justification: Government agencies legally responsible for oversight and control are unable to perform this function efficiently and effectively because they lack records of illegal mining locations. Therefore, it is crucial that technology, through a satellite monitoring system, assist in these governmental functions. Furthermore, the Mining Authority (AJAM) would need to share information from the Mining Cadastre and Grid, as well as the Mining Registry, to facilitate the identification of illegal mining using satellite systems.
Bolivia_Sources: - No aplicable.
Brazil_Justification:
Brazil possesses advanced governmental satellite monitoring systems, operated primarily by the National Institute for Space Research (INPE), the Management and Operational Center of the Amazon Protection System (CENSIPAM), and the Brazilian Institute of Environment and Renewable Natural Resources (IBAMA), which allow for the detection and monitoring of the expansion of mining areas, including gold mining in the Legal Amazon.

Among the main active governmental systems are:

DETER-B (Real-Time Deforestation Detection – INPE)

DETER-B, maintained by INPE within the scope of the Amazon Program (TerraBrasilis), uses medium-resolution satellite images (Sentinel-2, MODIS, and WFI/CBERS-4) to detect areas of altered forest cover, classifying them into categories such as "deforestation," "mining," "selective logging," and "degradation."

The "mining" category allows for the identification of active illegal mining operations and the expansion of mined areas, serving as a basis for enforcement actions by IBAMA (Brazilian Institute of Environment and Renewable Natural Resources) and the Federal Police.

However, the system does not formally distinguish between artisanal, semi-mechanized, and mechanized mining, since its resolution (10–30 m) and scope are geared towards detecting land use and land cover, and not the operational characterization of activities.

SIPAMSAR / CENSIPAM (Integrated Amazon Monitoring System)

CENSIPAM, an agency linked to the Ministry of Defense, operates the SIPAMSAR system, which integrates optical and radar (SAR) images with field data to monitor illegal activities in remote areas, including illegal mining in riverbeds and protected areas.

The system provides operational alerts to security forces (Army, IBAMA, and Federal Police) and supports the Amazon Plan 2023–2026.

Although it can detect distinct patterns of dredges and river mining fronts, there is still no published governmental technical protocol that systematically classifies the different types of mining (artisanal, semi-mechanized, or mechanized).

IBAMA Environmental Inspection System (GEO-IBAMA)

IBAMA maintains the GEO-IBAMA system, which integrates DETER-B alerts, PRODES data (annual deforestation monitoring), and contracted high-resolution images (PlanetScope, SkySat, and Amazonia-1) to plan environmental inspection operations.

The system allows for the identification of the size of mined areas and evidence of mechanization (such as access roads, clearings, and the presence of machinery), but this distinction depends on human analysis and contextual interpretation, not on automatic classification.

Sectoral Thematic Systems (FUNAI, Ministry of Mines and Energy, ANM)

FUNAI operates territorial monitoring modules in Indigenous Lands, in cooperation with CENSIPAM and INPE, which identify active mining operations and support actions to protect affected communities.

The National Mining Agency (ANM), in partnership with the Ministry of Mines and Energy (MME), uses DETER-B data and remote sensing to cross-reference valid mining titles and areas of illegal exploitation, but without operational differentiation between mining scales.

Therefore, Brazilian government satellite monitoring systems are effective in detecting mining areas, allowing for rapid and georeferenced responses by enforcement agencies. However, the automated distinction between artisanal, semi-mechanized, and mechanized mining is not yet technically consolidated or institutionally standardized. This differentiation depends on subsequent interpretive analysis by specialists and field inspection, and is not a native capability of official sensing systems.

CENSIPAM: https://www.gov.br/censipam/pt-br
GEO-IBAMA: https://www.gov.br/ibama
Colombia_Justification:
Governmental satellite and geospatial monitoring systems in Colombia lack the technical capacity to automatically distinguish between artisanal, semi-mechanized, and mechanized mining activities based solely on satellite imagery.

The IDEAM Forest and Carbon Monitoring System (SMByC) identifies changes in forest cover and detects vegetation loss associated with gold mining, especially in regions such as the Amazon, Chocó, and Bajo Cauca. However, the system focuses on detecting disturbed areas rather than on the technical characterization of mining activity.

Similarly, reports derived from satellite analysis, such as the Alluvial Gold Mining Report (EVOA) prepared by the UNODC in cooperation with the Ministry of Mines and Energy and the Ministry of Environment, identify the extent of affected areas and the spatial patterns of mining, but require field verification or manual interpretation to differentiate between artisanal, semi-mechanized, and mechanized mining.

To date, Colombia does not have automated algorithms or national platforms that integrate technical indicators (e.g., type of machinery, size of infrastructure or removal density) that allow the classification of the operational scale of mining based on satellite analysis.

Peru_Justification: In Peru, authorities and projects (e.g., MINEM/MINAM together with SERVIR/NASA, MAAP, OEFA, and academic groups) integrate satellite alerts with verification to prioritize inspections. In practice, they typically use continuous/near-real-time detection to locate new disturbances and then differentiate their types using higher-resolution images and verification. Satellite monitoring systems can detect the presence of machinery and the scale of the operation, allowing inferences to be made about whether it is artisanal, semi-mechanized, or mechanized mining. However, it is not always possible to make this distinction accurately, especially in remote and difficult-to-access areas.

Question: Is mercury concentration measured by the government in potentially affected water bodies, the food chain and/or populations?
Bolivia_Justification: The government has not conducted monitoring studies on mercury concentration in water bodies and populations. What does exist are investigations carried out by NGOs and universities, whose results have been used to create the Baseline on Uses, Emissions, and Pollution, a document prepared by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and the Ministry of Environment and Water. The Mercury and Health Plan aims to establish strategic guidelines to direct the actions of the health sector toward reducing the risks and effects resulting from mercury exposure in the Bolivian population. The Technical Guide for the Prevention, Diagnosis, Treatment, and Surveillance of Mercury Poisoning in Bolivia aims to strengthen the knowledge of health personnel regarding the prevention, diagnosis, treatment, and surveillance of mercury poisoning in Bolivia.

- Ministerio de Relaciones Exteriores & Ministerio de Medio Ambiente y Agua (2015). Mercurio en Bolivia: Línea Base de Usos, Emisiones y Contaminación. Disponible en: https://es.scribd.com/document/629011811/2014-LIBRO-MERCURIO-EN-BOLIVIA-2014
- Guia Técnica para la Prevención, Diagnóstico, Tratamiento y Vigilancia de la Intoxicación por Mercurio en Bolivia. Brazil_Justification:
Brazil does not have a continuous, integrated, and nationally coordinated government program for the systematic monitoring of mercury contamination in water bodies, the food chain, and exposed human populations, especially in areas of artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM). However, there are efforts and specific programs conducted by federal and state public agencies, often in partnership with research and health institutions, that carry out periodic measurements and surveys.

Several studies and reports highlight the problem:

Oswaldo Cruz Foundation (Fiocruz) – linked to the Ministry of Health
Fiocruz coordinates, in cooperation with the Special Secretariat for Indigenous Health (SESAI), several epidemiological studies and biomonitoring of mercury in indigenous and riverside populations in the Amazon.

A 2016 survey, conducted in partnership with the Socio-Environmental Institute (ISA), found mercury contamination levels exceeding 90% among the Yanomami in areas impacted by mining.

In 2019, a study by Fiocruz found that 56% of indigenous people in Maturacá and Ariabu (Amazonas) had mercury levels above the WHO limit (2 μg/g of hair).
These studies, while relevant, are isolated and reactive, carried out as epidemiological investigations and not as part of a permanent environmental and public health monitoring system.

National Foundation for Indigenous Peoples (FUNAI) and Ministry of Health (SESAI)
In indigenous areas, especially in the Yanomami Territory, the federal government initiated an emergency health and environmental remediation plan in 2023, which includes punctual collections of biological and water samples to assess mercury contamination.

These actions were intensified in the context of the Yanomami humanitarian crisis, but do not constitute a permanent biomonitoring program.

International Partnerships and Integrated Data
Brazil participates in regional projects supported by the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) and the Amazon Cooperation Treaty Organization (ACTO) to develop methodologies for measuring mercury in fish and sediments; however, the data are still collected experimentally and in a decentralized manner.

In summary, although the problem of mercury contamination is well documented by scientific and non-governmental organizations, and Brazil is a signatory to international agreements, there is no explicit evidence from the sources consulted that the Brazilian government systematically measures mercury concentration in the specified areas as a direct and continuous program related to MAPE.

Colombia_Justification:
The Colombian government conducts mercury measurements, but these are limited, unsystematic, and have only partial coverage. While there are significant institutional efforts, a comprehensive and continuous national monitoring system encompassing various environmental matrices (water, air, soil, food) and the effects on human health is still lacking.

The National Institute of Health (INS) has conducted epidemiological and biomonitoring studies in mining and riverine populations in regions such as Antioquia, Bolívar, Chocó, and Cauca, revealing high levels of mercury in blood and hair. However, these studies have been carried out sporadically and without a set schedule, thus failing to constitute a sustained national surveillance network.

The Institute of Hydrology, Meteorology and Environmental Studies (IDEAM), along with some Regional Autonomous Corporations (CARs), conducts water quality monitoring that includes mercury measurements in basins affected by gold mining (for example, the Atrato, Magdalena, and Caquetá rivers). Despite this, the reports are fragmented, heterogeneous, and dependent on regional operational capacity.

The National Action Plan (NAP) on Mercury in Small-Scale Mining (SAM), developed by the Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development (MinAmbiente) in compliance with the Minamata Convention, itself acknowledges this institutional weakness. In its Strategy 3 (Emissions Reduction), the NAP proposes “mapping and monitoring mercury-contaminated sites” and “conducting studies on the presence of mercury in environmental matrices.” Likewise, Strategy 9 (Public Health) proposes “monitoring the reporting of mercury poisoning cases” and consolidating information to identify exposed populations.

These actions demonstrate official recognition of the problem and a commitment to moving toward a more structured inter-institutional monitoring system, although its implementation is still underway.

Peru_Justification: Yes, the Peruvian government does conduct official mercury measurements at various points in the supply chain, although coverage is fragmented and the frequency varies. The Ministry of the Environment (MINAM) publishes inventories and studies on mercury stocks, emissions, and releases, providing information on mercury sources and levels in the country. The Environmental Assessment and Enforcement Agency (OEFA) conducts environmental monitoring of water, sediment, and soil in mining-affected areas and publishes field reports and analytical results (spot sampling related to inspections and campaigns). The Ministry of Health (MINSA) has conducted health assessments and biomedical sampling in exposed populations (blood/urine mercury levels, screenings) and disseminates public health recommendations. However, these measurements are not systematic or periodic, nor do they cover all areas affected by gold mining. The lack of a national mercury monitoring program hinders the assessment of the true impact of mercury contamination in the country.

Question: Are there legal channels for civil society oversight or participation in monitoring and enforcement?
Bolivia_Justification:
Law No. 1333, in Articles 92 to 94, recognizes the importance of citizen participation in environmental management, as well as the right to be informed truthfully and promptly. Likewise, the Political Constitution of the State (CPE) establishes that the population has the right to participate in environmental management (Article 343), including social participation in the care of water resources (Article 374, paragraph I).

Public consultation procedures exist for environmental licensing processes, provided the project is classified as Category 1 or 2. This means the public consultation must be carried out within the framework of the Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) in accordance with the Environmental Prevention and Control Regulations (Article 162). Public hearings (Articles 79 to 82) are also required, in accordance with the General Environmental Management Regulations. Furthermore, Law No. 341 on Participation and Social Control establishes in Article 8, point 13, that the population has the right to participate in environmental management and to be consulted and informed in advance about decisions that could affect the quality of the environment and the conservation of ecosystems. Point 15 further indicates the right to participate in the entire mining production chain.

Only in the hydrocarbon sector is there specific legislation for socio-environmental monitoring, namely Supreme Decree No. 29103, which applies only in the territories of indigenous peoples and peasant communities.

- Ley Nº 341 de Partición y Control Social. Disponible en: https://sea.gob.bo/digesto/CompendioII/R/197_L_341.pdf
Brazil_Justification:
In Brazil, the legal channels for civil society participation in the supervision, monitoring, and oversight of artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) are not explicitly defined or robustly implemented in specific legal frameworks for ASM. Although the Brazilian Constitution generally guarantees the freedoms of association, peaceful assembly, and expression, and the legal framework broadly supports the exercise of these rights, direct and specific legal channels for civil society oversight of ASM are limited.

Previously, Decree 10.966/2022, of February 11, 2022, established the Program to Support the Development of Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining (Pró-Mape) and an Interministerial Commission for the Development of Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining (Comape). However, this decree was revoked by Decree 11,369/2023, of January 1, 2023. Under Decree 10,966/2022, civil society participation in the Comape was restricted to invitations to meetings without voting rights, indicating a limited formal channel for direct involvement. With its revocation, even this limited and specific formal channel for MAPE ceased to exist.

The "Action Plan for the Review of the Independent Reporting Mechanism: Brazil 2023–2027" highlights Brazil's commitments to promoting transparency and citizen oversight, especially in infrastructure projects. While this indicates a broader governmental recognition of the importance of civil society engagement and transparency, it does not specifically address MAPE.

In summary, although there is a general constitutional right to participation and some broader initiatives for transparency and citizen oversight, specific and effective legal channels for civil society to oversee, monitor, and audit MAPE in Brazil are absent or have been dismantled.

Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has multiple legal channels for civil society participation in monitoring and oversight. The 1991 Constitution establishes the right to citizen participation, and Law 134 of 1994 regulates the mechanisms for citizen participation [8].
Citizen oversight committees are regulated by Law 850 of 2003, which allows citizens to monitor public administration [9]. Furthermore, Law 1712 of 2014 guarantees access to public information [10].

The Escazú Agreement (approved in Colombia by Law 2273 of 2022) strengthens and expands the existing legal channels in the country. It provides three fundamental pillars:
1. Access to Environmental Information
2. Public Participation in Environmental Decisions
3. Access to Justice and Protection for Environmental Defenders

Peru_Justification: Yes, legal channels exist for civil society participation in environmental monitoring and oversight. In any impact study (EIA, semi-detailed EIA, etc.), processes for public information and participation must be opened (calls for participation, hearings, access to files). Furthermore, the OEFA (Environmental Assessment and Enforcement Agency) has the Regulations for Citizen Participation in Environmental Monitoring Actions, approved by Board Resolution No. 032-2014-OEFA/CD and subsequent amendments. The OEFA also operates the National Environmental Information and Complaints Service (SINADA) and the PIFA Portal, where citizens can file complaints, track their status, and access information on inspections. In addition, the General Environmental Law (Law No. 28611) recognizes the right of citizens to participate in environmental management.

Question: Is there an official, accessible, safe, and functional channel for individuals or communities to file complaints related to ASGM activities or associated environmental impacts?
Bolivia_Justification:
It should be clarified that environmental regulations establish a general procedure for handling complaints, which is set forth in Article 101 of Law No. 1333, as well as in the General Environmental Management Regulations (Articles 83 to 85).

Law No. 535 establishes the suspension of work at the request of the Environmental Authority (AAC) when complaints regarding environmental impact are verified (Article 103, paragraph a)). In the case of granting mining rights, the Regulations for Granting and Terminating Mining Rights establish procedures for filing complaints to nullify the right (Article 57) as well as in cases of opposition (Article 22).

- Reglamento de Otorgación y Extinción de Derechos Mineros. Disponible en: https://www.autoridadminera.gob.bo/index.php/reglamentos-2/
Brazil_Justification:
Yes. Brazil has official, accessible, secure, and functional channels for individuals and communities to file complaints, although there is no channel exclusively dedicated to MAPE activities and their environmental impacts. The main channel is Fala.BR, a platform of the Comptroller General of the Union (CGU), used to send complaints, grievances, and reports to the federal public administration. Through this system, it is possible to direct a complaint directly to the National Mining Agency (ANM) or other competent bodies.

For complaints related to environmental infractions, IBAMA offers the Linha Verde service, which receives reports on deforestation, illegal mining, and other environmental crimes. The service can be accessed free of charge by calling 0800 061 8080 or through the electronic form available in the "Talk to IBAMA" section of the institutional website.

In addition, the Federal Public Prosecutor's Office (MPF) maintains its own channels for receiving complaints of environmental crimes, including cases of illegal mining. The Federal Public Prosecutor's Office (MPF) plays a central role in investigating and holding offenders accountable and in repairing environmental damage, working in partnership with regulatory bodies and the Federal Police.

Colombia_Justification:
Yes. Colombia has several official, accessible, and secure channels for reporting artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) activities or their environmental impacts, managed by justice, oversight, and environmental agencies.

The Attorney General's Office has an Environmental Unit that receives reports of illegal mining, pollution, or mercury trafficking through the 122 hotline, the citizen portal, and local offices.

The Office of the Inspector General offers the Online Complaints and Reports System (www.procuraduria.gov.co), which guarantees confidentiality and digital tracking.
The National Environmental Licensing Authority (ANLA) has an online form (www.anla.gov.co/denuncias-ambientales), and the Regional Autonomous Corporations (CARs) handle reports of environmental damage or illegal mining through their institutional websites (e.g., CORPOAMAZONIA). The National Mining Agency (ANM) receives alerts about irregular or unlicensed activities through its Citizen Service System (SAC) (www.anm.gov.co).

Finally, the Ministry of Environment maintains the national hotline 01 8000 11 3340 to combat wildlife trafficking, and the National Police allows environmental complaints to be filed through the 123 hotline or the “ADenunciar” app.

Together, these channels guarantee citizen access, confidentiality, and traceability of environmental complaints, including those related to artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM).

Through this portal, anyone can file a complaint about a suspected environmental violation. OEFA is obligated to investigate these complaints and take appropriate action.

Question: Are there processes for public consultation with affected communities in mining-related decisions?
Bolivia_Justification:
Public consultation is conducted within the framework of the technical and administrative procedure for obtaining an environmental license. Specifically, when an Environmental Operations Group (EOG) obtains Category 1 or 2 status, it is obligated to conduct an Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA), which in turn requires a public consultation.

The public consultation procedure is not exclusive to the mining sector but applies to any EOG required to conduct an EIA (Art. 162 of the Environmental Prevention and Control Regulations).

Law No. 535 does not establish public consultation. Instead, it addresses prior, free, and informed consultation, which is mandated by the Bolivian Constitution (CPE) in cases where non-renewable natural resources or activities affect indigenous communities. Therefore, there is no process or procedure for public consultation in mining decisions.

Furthermore, it is important to remember that Bolivia is a signatory to the Escazú Agreement, which recognizes citizen participation as a right.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Reglamento de Prevención y Control Ambiental. Disponible en: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/modulos/Biblioteca/items/03092021-010949-532781_209/03092021-010949-532781_209.pdf
- Ley Nª 535 de Minería y Metalurgia. Disponible en: https://www.comibol.gob.bo/ images/noticias/Ley_535.pdf
- Acuerdo de Escazú. Disponible en: https://acuerdodeescazu.org/derechos/ y https://repositorio.cepal.org/server/api/core/bitstreams/a6049491-a9ee-4c53-ae7c-a8a17ca9504e/content
Brazil_Justification:
Yes. In Brazil, environmental legislation requires public hearings as part of the environmental licensing process for projects with significant environmental impact, such as mining. This requirement aims to guarantee social participation and transparency in decision-making that affects local communities and the environment.

CONAMA Resolution No. 09/1987 establishes the guidelines for holding public hearings, defining their objectives, methods of convening, and stages. According to Article 1, the purpose of the hearing is "to present the content of the Environmental Impact Report (EIR) to interested parties, clarify doubts, and gather criticisms and suggestions." Article 3 stipulates that the hearing must be conducted by the responsible environmental agency, guaranteeing space for all interested parties to express their views.

Furthermore, Brazil is a signatory to Convention No. 169 of the International Labour Organization (ILO), ratified by Decree No. 5,051/2004 and consolidated by Decree No. 10,088/2019, which ensures indigenous peoples and traditional communities the right to prior, free and informed consultation (FPIC) on administrative or legislative measures that may directly affect them (Articles 6 and 15). However, the implementation of this right still faces challenges, such as delays and lack of representativeness in consultation processes.

In 2019, IBAMA published Draft Normative Instruction No. 4,206,327, which defines three modalities of social participation:
(i) Public Hearing, in person and open to any interested party, applied to projects subject to EIA/RIMA;
(ii) Informative Technical Meeting, aimed at projects not subject to EIA/RIMA; (iii) Public Consultation, of a consultative nature, to obtain contributions from society in IBAMA's administrative processes.

Despite the regulatory framework, the practical effectiveness of these mechanisms is still limited. Many hearings are conducted in a perfunctory manner, with low inclusion of affected communities and little influence of social contributions on final decisions.

Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has mandatory public consultation processes for mining decisions. Prior consultation for Indigenous and Afro-Colombian communities is established in the Constitution and regulated by Decree 1320 of 1998 [14].
For environmental licenses, Decree 1076 of 2015 establishes processes for citizen participation and public hearings [15]. Law 99 of 1993 also establishes mechanisms for participation in environmental decisions [16].

Peru_Justification: Yes, Peru does have formal public consultation processes with communities when decisions (laws, regulations, plans, programs, or projects) may affect collective rights or the environment, but these processes vary depending on the type of community and the applicable law. For Indigenous or native peoples, there is the right to prior consultation, regulated by Law No. 29785 and its implementing regulations, Supreme Decree No. 001-2012-MC, which establishes the obligation to consult with Indigenous peoples about mining projects that may affect their collective rights. For projects subject to the Environmental Impact Assessment System (SEIA/EIA) (for example, many mining projects), Law 27446 requires citizen participation plans and public hearings at the various stages of the Environmental Impact Study; SENACE and the SEIA regulate and operate these mechanisms.
Peru_Sources:
Ley del derecho a la consulta previa a los pueblos indígenas u originarios, reconocido en el Convenio 169 de la Organización Internacional del Trabajo (OIT): https://spij.minjus.gob.pe/spij-ext-web/#/detallenorma/H1039114
Ley N° 27446: https://spij.minjus.gob.pe/spij-ext-web/#/detallenorma/H806054
Ecuador_Justification: Public consultation processes in mining decisions are framed within the constitutional right to citizen participation and are outlined in Articles 89 and 90 of the Mining Law, which provide for: (i) a consultation process with the community, and (ii) a special consultation process with indigenous communities, peoples, and nationalities. This classification is consistent with that established by the Constitution and the Organic Environmental Code, which has been characterized in case law as: (i) environmental consultation, as a diffuse environmental right; and (ii) free, prior, and informed consultation, as a collective right of indigenous peoples and nationalities. Environmental consultation is regulated by Decree 754, which amends the Regulations to the Organic Environmental Code; this is complemented by Article 24 of the Environmental Regulations for Mining Activities. Prior consultation is regulated by Agreement MEM-MEM-2024-0002-AM, which contains the Manual for the operationalization of free and informed prior consultation, contained in number 7 of article 57 of the Constitution of the Republic of Ecuador for the issuance of administrative measures in mining concessions.
Ecuador_Sources:
Ley de Minería.
Decreto 754.
Acuerdo MEM-MEM-2024-0002-AM.
Corte Constitucional. Sentencia 51-23-IN/23.

Question: Are civil society complaints taken into account in the authorization or assessment processes of mining projects?
Bolivia_Justification:
In addition to the procedures established in environmental regulations regarding public consultation, public hearings, and information requests, there are also complaint mechanisms available through the Ombudsman's Office.

Furthermore, another legal mechanism established in the Constitution (Articles 135 and 136) is the class action lawsuit, which allows citizens to actively participate in protecting collective rights and interests related to heritage, the environment, public space, safety, and public health, among others.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Defensoria del Pueblo al servicio de la ciudadanía – Atención de quejas y denunicas (2022). Disponible en: https://www.defensoria.gob.bo/uploads/files/atencion-de-quejas-y-denuncias.pdf
- Constitución Política del Estado. Disponible en: https://diputados.gob.bo/wp-content/uploads/2022/02/cpe.pdf
- Reglamento General de Gestión Ambiental. Disponible en: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/modulos/Biblioteca/items/03092021-010949-532781_209/03092021-010949-532781_209.pdf
- Ley Nº 1333. Disponible en: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/modulos/Biblioteca/items/03092021-010949-532781_209/03092021-010949-532781_209.pdf
- Ley Nº 535 de Minería y Metalurgia. Disponible en: https://www.comibol.gob.bo/ images/noticias/Ley_535.pdf
- Reglamento de Otorgación y Extinción de Derechos Mineros. Disponible en: https://www.autoridadminera.gob.bo/index.php/reglamentos-2/
Brazil_Justification:
Yes, complaints from civil society can be considered in the authorization and evaluation processes of mining projects in Brazil. Based on received complaints, the Public Prosecutor's Office can initiate civil investigations to investigate irregularities and, if necessary, file lawsuits to suspend or annul illegally granted mining licenses.

Furthermore, complaints can support the actions of environmental agencies, such as IBAMA, in the inspection of mining projects. IBAMA can conduct inspections and issue fines to companies that are violating environmental legislation.

IBAMA, through its Green Line (https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/denuncias), and the National Mining Agency (ANM), via the Federal Government's General Ombudsman (https://www.gov.br/anm), receive complaints and petitions that can be incorporated into mining evaluation and inspection processes.
Brazil_Sources:
http://www.mpf.mp.br/
https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br
Colombia_Justification:
Complaints and observations from civil society are considered in mining and environmental authorization processes in Colombia. Decree 1076 of 2015 establishes that environmental authorities must consider citizen observations in the environmental licensing evaluation process [15].
Law 1437 of 2011 (Administrative Procedure Code) establishes the right to participate in administrative proceedings [17].
Colombia_Sources:

•Código de Procedimiento Administrativo y de lo Contencioso Administrativo
Peru_Justification: Yes, complaints and participation from civil society are considered in the evaluation and authorization processes for mining projects in Peru: legal mechanisms exist for receiving and registering public comments on Environmental Impact Assessments (EIAs) (SENACE/SEIA), and administrative channels (SINADA/OEFA) inform oversight and the prioritization of inspections. While channels exist for filing complaints, it is not always clear to what extent these complaints influence decision-making regarding the authorization of mining projects. The lack of transparency in this process hinders the evaluation of the real impact of citizen participation.

Ecuador_Justification: Complaints are filed when an alleged administrative or criminal violation is discovered, which is independent of the authorization or evaluation of mining projects. Therefore, Ecuadorian law does not foresee any effect of a complaint on the administrative procedure for authorizing or evaluating a project.

Question: Do national programs on ASM include specific measures to support the participation of women, Indigenous peoples, or traditional communities?
Bolivia_Justification:
Mining regulations do not establish specific measures for women and Indigenous people in artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), given that ASM is neither defined nor characterized in Law No. 535.

This law establishes prior consultation as a fundamental right of Indigenous, peasant, and intercultural communities, as well as the Afro-Bolivian people (Art. 207).
Bolivia_Sources: - Ley Nº 535 de Minería y Metalurgia. Disponible en: https://www.comibol.gob.bo/ images/noticias/Ley_535.pdf
Brazil_Justification:
In Brazil, there is currently no structured and effective national program dedicated exclusively to the inclusion and participation of women, indigenous peoples, and traditional communities that incorporates specific and continuous measures to promote the inclusion and participation of women, indigenous peoples, and traditional communities.

However, the National Policy Plan for Women (National Secretariat for Policies for Women / Ministry of Women) and the National Plan for Traditional Peoples and Communities (Ministry of Agrarian Development and Family Agriculture) recognize the role of rural and traditional women in extractive and community-based productive activities, which could include artisanal mining in specific contexts.
Brazil_Sources:
[2] CIMI. (2022). Relatório de Violência Contra os Povos Indígenas no Brasil. Disponível em: https://cimi.org.br/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/relatorio-violencia-contra-os-povos-indigenas-no-brasil-2021-cimi.pdf
Secretaria Nacional de Políticas para as Mulheres – Plano Nacional de Políticas para as Mulheres 2024–2027.
Disponível em: https://www.gov.br/mulheres
Colombia_Justification:
National programs on artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) in Colombia include some elements of a differential approach, but not comprehensively. Law 2250 of 2022 recognizes traditional mining and establishes differentiated procedures, but does not include specific, detailed measures for women, Indigenous peoples, or traditional communities [1].
Some programs, such as the Better Gold Initiative, include criteria for gender equity and community participation, but they are not official national programs [2].

Peru_Justification: Yes, national programs on small-scale and artisanal mining (ASM) include measures to support the participation of women, Indigenous peoples, and communities. The Ministry of Energy and Mines (MINEM) approved a National Gender Plan for Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining (2023-2025) through Ministerial Resolution No. 524-2023-MINEM/DM, which incorporates awareness-raising measures, training, mainstreaming of a gender perspective in planning instruments, empowerment initiatives, and actions such as the "Magic Women" program and support for networks of women miners. Furthermore, the National Multisectoral Policy for Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining to 2030 (approved by Supreme Decree No. 016-2022-EM) mandates the coordination of actions among entities and the provision of territorial and intersectoral interventions that must consider local populations and social aspects. However, implementation is uneven, as coverage, budget, regional capacities, and inter-institutional coordination vary by region, and many measures depend on cooperation projects for their execution.

Ecuador_Justification: In 2023, the Organic Law to Promote the Violet Economy came into effect. Its objective is to strengthen, promote, guarantee, and implement the mainstreaming of a gender and multicultural approach through the creation of incentives and public policies that encourage the civilian population to empower women in all their diversity, especially those who have been victims of violence or are in vulnerable situations, including women from different peoples and nationalities. At the beginning of 2025, a survey was conducted analyzing 14 private companies in the sector, which determined that female participation increased from 16.95% in 2021 to 19.56% in 2025, surpassing countries like Peru (6%) and Colombia (13%).

Question: Are there protocols for the care and protection of victims and witnesses of crimes related to or derived from illegal mining? *0.5 may be given in cases there are general protection of witnesses and victims programs, but not specific for illegal mining
Bolivia_Justification: The Bolivian Attorney General's Office has a Directorate for the Protection of Victims, Witnesses, and Members of the Public Prosecutor's Office (DPVTMMP), responsible for implementing the Protection Program established by Law 458. This directorate, created by the Organic Law of the Public Prosecutor's Office No. 260/2012 (Art. 90), coordinates protection and assistance policies at the national level. Furthermore, Law No. 458/2013 on the Protection of Whistleblowers and Witnesses establishes specific measures to safeguard individuals who cooperate in criminal proceedings, laying the groundwork for current protection programs. There is still no regulation or protocol specifically for victims of illegal mining.
Bolivia_Sources:
Ley Nº 458/2013
Ley Orgánica del Ministerio Público Nº 260/2012
Brazil_Justification:
Brazil was a pioneer in South America in establishing a framework for witness protection: Law 9807/1999 created the Federal Program for Assistance to Victims and Witnesses, known as PROVITA. In recent years, in the face of the Yanomami humanitarian crisis caused by illegal mining in the Amazon, the Brazilian government has reinforced these measures: in 2025, the Ministry of Human Rights allocated extraordinary resources to adapt PROVITA to the collective protection of indigenous communities threatened by illegal miners.

Furthermore, the Federal Public Prosecutor's Office created a specialized unit to combat illegal mining in the Amazon, which coordinates prevention and repression actions, and includes the protection of indigenous witnesses who denounce miners on their lands.
Brazil_Sources: https://www.gov.br/mdh/pt-br/navegue-por-temas/pessoas-ameacadas-de-morte/acoes-e-programas/programa-de-protecao-a-vitimas-e-testemunhas-ameacadas-provita/legislacao#:~:text=Lei%209,de%20acusados%20ou%20condenados%20que
Colombia_Justification: Colombia has a state-coordinated Victim and Witness Protection Program. Initially conceived under the Justice and Peace Law, this program is coordinated with the National Protection Unit (UNP) and the Attorney General's Office, and seeks to safeguard the life, integrity, liberty, and security of individuals threatened for cooperating with the justice system. While Colombia has protection protocols and specialized forces to assist those threatened for reporting or testifying against criminal networks, there is no specific protocol or legislation for illegal mining networks.

https://www.policia.gov.co/noticia/hacemos-frente-mineria-ilegal#:~:text=La%20operaci%C3%B3n%20liderada%20por%20el,la%20extracci%C3%B3n%20ilegal%20del%20mineral

https://savvialegal.com/2024/05/16/articulo-de-opinion-la-mineria-ilegal-y-el-lavado-de-activos-en-colombia/#:~:text=capacidades%20para%20detectar%20operaciones%20sospechosas,pol%C3%ADtica%20al%20m%C3%A1s%20alto%20nivel
Peru_Justification:
In 2013, Law No. 30077 against Organized Crime included illegal mining as a crime eligible for investigation using special techniques (when three or more organized individuals are involved), allowing the use of mechanisms such as cooperating witnesses, lifting of bank secrecy, and enhanced witness protection in these proceedings.

In Madre de Dios, the Public Prosecutor's Office has multidisciplinary teams to assist women rescued from trafficking networks operating in brothels within mining camps, providing them with temporary shelter, therapy, and legal guidance. In 2016, the Public Prosecutor's Office approved a "Protocol for the Investigation of Illegal Mining Interdiction Cases," which details the procedure from the planning stage of the operation to the trial phase.

Additionally, in 2020-2021, the Public Prosecutor's Office developed a Protocol for the Care of Victims Rescued in Operations against Illegal Mining, focusing specifically on victims of human trafficking.
Peru_Sources:
https://www.rumbominero.com/peru/noticias/mineria/fiscalia-ya-cuenta-con-protocolo-para-la-indagacion-de-casos-de-mineria-ilegal/#:~:text=interdicci%C3%B3n%20de%20miner%C3%ADa%20ilegal%2C%20siendo,estas%20de%20programaci%C3%B3n%20e

https://img.lpderecho.pe/wp-content/uploads/2016/11/Resolucion-N%C2%BA-4269-2016-MP-FN-Protocolo-para-la-realizaci%C3%B3n-de-Operativos-de-Interdicci%C3%B3n-contra-la-Miner%C3%ADa-Ilegal.pdf
Ecuador_Justification: The Attorney General's Office of Ecuador manages the National System for the Protection and Assistance of Victims, Witnesses, and Other Participants (SPAVT). This system, established under the Organic Code of the Judiciary and the Comprehensive Organic Criminal Code (COIP), has 23 specialized units nationwide, responsible for assessing risks and implementing protection measures. When a victim or witness of a crime (for example, a witness to illegal mining who reports a criminal gang) is threatened, the SPAVT activates measures ranging from psychological support and legal counsel to semi-permanent or permanent police protection. However, Ecuador still lacks a formal protection system specifically for victims of mining.

Question: Are there public programs to support formalization through incentives to and capacity-building of ASM gold miners?
Bolivia_Justification:
Bolivia does not have public formalization programs for artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM). Law No. 535 provides guidelines for obtaining mining rights, the detailed procedure for which is found in the Regulations for Granting and Terminating Mining Rights.

The Mining and Metallurgical Authority (AJAM) does not provide technical assistance; the Geological, Mining and Mining Service (SERGEOMIN) offers paid technical assistance.

The PlanetGOLD Bolivia project, implemented by the United Nations Industrial Development Organization (UNIDO) and executed by Projekt-Consult GmbH, will come into effect in 2023. Its strategic partners are the Ministry of Mining and Metallurgy, the Ministry of Environment and Water, and the Ministry of Health and Sports. One of PlanetGOLD's components is the formalization of ASM.
Bolivia_Sources:
- SERGEOMIN. https://www.sergeomin.gob.bo/index.php/prospeccion-y-exploracion/
- PlanetGOLD Bolivia. https://www.planetgold.org/es/bolivia
Brazil_Justification:
Yes, Brazil has public programs that support formalization through incentives and capacity building for artisanal and small-scale gold miners in the MAPE (Ministry of Agriculture, Livestock and Supply). The Program to Support the Development of Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining (Pró-Mape), the Artisanal Mining Permit (PLG), and the "ASGM – Coexistence in Brazil" project from USP (University of São Paulo) are clear examples of these initiatives, which aim at regularization, the adoption of best practices, and the training of artisanal miners.

Pró-Mape, established by Decree No. 10,966/2022, aims to integrate and strengthen public policies for the sustainable development of artisanal and small-scale mining. The program seeks to promote best practices, formalization, and cooperation, as well as encourage the health, assistance, and dignity of the communities involved.

The Artisanal Mining Permit (PLG) is a regime for the extraction of mineral substances with immediate exploitation of the mineral deposit that, due to its nature, size, location, and economic use, can be mined independently of prior research work, according to criteria established by the National Mining Agency (ANM). The PLG is an important instrument for formalizing artisanal mining activity.

Disponível em: https://www.gov.br/mme/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/esclarecimentos-sobre-o-programa-de-apoio-ao-desenvolvimento-da-mineracao-artesanal-e-em-pequena-escala-e-aprimoramento-do-codigo-de-mineracao

Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has specific public programs to support the formalization of artisanal and small-scale (ASS) miners. Law 2250 of 2022 establishes a special framework for mining legalization and formalization, including differentiated procedures and support programs [1].
The Ministry of Mines and Energy has developed formalization programs that include technical assistance, training, and support for obtaining mining titles and environmental licenses. In addition, the country participates in the Better Gold Initiative, which provides comprehensive technical assistance to mining organizations [2].

Peru_Justification: Yes, the Peruvian government has implemented programs to support the formalization of artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM). The Ministry of Energy and Mines (MINEM) maintains an official formalization program that includes training, technical assistance, and administrative support to help miners access the REINFO (National Registry of Informal Mining Operators) and complete their formalization process. MINEM has also promoted initiatives to facilitate access to credit and improved marketing channels for formalized miners (through announcements and pilot projects). Furthermore, the government announced the creation of a mining fund to support small-scale mining with financing at preferential rates, technical assistance, and marketing channels. The proposed new ASM Law also includes incentives for formalization.

Within this framework, the PlanetGOLD Ecuador project, for example, aims to transform artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) by promoting sustainable practices and reducing mercury use. This project, led by the national environmental authority and supported by the UNDP and the GEF, focuses specifically on formalizing the ASM sector, including improving the integration of artisanal and small-scale mining into the formal regulatory framework and providing capacity building.

Question: Are there accessible financing mechanisms promoted by the State to support sustainable practices as an alternative to unsustainable ASM gold mining?
Bolivia_Justification: The Mining Financing Fund (FOFIM) is a public entity responsible for granting loans to mining cooperatives to promote and support projects for improving exploitation and concentration systems, purchasing concentration plants, marketing minerals, and acquiring equipment and/or machinery; thus enabling the optimization of productivity indices in their production. However, the signing of an inter-institutional agreement for responsible mining between FOFIM and PlanetGOLD Bolivia is recent.
Bolivia_Sources: - La Razón (2025). Gobierno firma convenio interinstitucional para minería responsable. Disponible en: https://larazon.bo/economia-y-empresa/2025/06/18/118382/
Brazil_Justification:
Brazil has some public financing and credit mechanisms aimed at sustainable ventures, which could, in theory, benefit artisanal miners or formal cooperatives of the MAPE (Mining and Agricultural Production System). However, there are no specific and widely accessible financing lines aimed at encouraging small-scale sustainable mining practices.

For example, there are financing funds managed by the Bank of the Amazon and the Bank of Brazil, such as the Constitutional Financing Fund for the North and Midwest. These funds offer credit for sustainable productive ventures in the Amazon region and the Midwest.

However, there is no direct financing to promote sustainable small-scale mining in Brazil. At an event in October 2025 held by the newspaper Correio Braziliense, the president of the Federation of Mining Cooperatives of the State of Mato Grosso, Gilson Camboin, stated that the organized artisanal mining sector seeks to strengthen legality and eliminate the use of mercury, but faces difficulties due to a lack of support and financing.

Furthermore, the Ministry of Mines and Energy (MME) is forging a partnership with the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) for a small-scale gold mining project. This initiative, announced on July 4, 2025, aims to promote safer and more sustainable practices in the sector, aligning with Brazil's commitments to the Minamata Convention.

Programs such as the PNIS's Illicit Crop Substitution Program include productive alternatives, but they are not specifically aimed at ASM miners.

Question: Are safer mercury-free technologies promoted through incentives or support programs?
Bolivia_Justification:
The National Action Plan (NAP) has not yet been approved and submitted to the Minamata Convention Secretariat, so it is incorrect to claim that this instrument has developed initiatives to promote safe and mercury-free technologies, as it is not yet an official document.

In the 1990s, the Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (SDC), through the MEDMIN Foundation, carried out a project focused on small-scale mining and reducing the environmental impacts caused by this activity. Its main objective was the implementation of clean technologies in mining operations to mitigate environmental damage.

With support from WCS Bolivia, responsible mining pilot projects have been conducted in the Apolobamba Protected Natural Area (ANMIN) and the Cotapata National Park (ANMI), introducing equipment such as gravimetric tables to reduce and/or eliminate the use of mercury.

Subsequently, from 2023 the PlanetGOLD Bolivia project, implemented by the United Nations Industrial Development Organization (UNIDO) and executed by Projekt-Consult GmbH, came into effect. Among the components of PlanetGOLD is "Improving the capture of mercury-free technologies".
Bolivia_Sources:
- Fundación MEDMIN. https://www.medmin.org/presentacion/
- PlanetGOLD Bolivia. https://www.planetgold.org/es/bolivia
- WCS Bolivia (2021). Cooperativas mineras implementan medidas de producción más limpia en la cuenca alta del rio Suches. Disponible en: https://bolivia.wcs.org/es-es/Recursos-Informativos/Sala-de-noticias/articleType/ArticleView/articleId/16780/Cooperativas-mineras-implementan-medidas-de-produccion-mas-limpia-en-la-cuenca-alta-del-rio-Suches.aspx
Brazil_Justification:
Yes, Brazil is actively promoting safer, mercury-free technologies in artisanal and small-scale gold mining (ASPM) through various incentives and support programs, driven primarily by its commitment to the Minamata Convention on Mercury. The most prominent initiative in this regard is the "Mercury-Free Gold Project."

This project, launched in November 2022, is a collaborative effort between Brazil's Ministry of Mines and Energy (MME), the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP), and is funded by the Global Environment Facility (GEF). Its main objective is to support Brazil in developing and implementing a National Action Plan to reduce and ultimately eliminate mercury pollution from gold extraction, with a specific focus on ASPM.

Furthermore, the Ministry of Mines and Energy (MM) is coordinating a partnership with the UN for a small-scale gold mining project. The initiative aims to promote safer and more sustainable practices in the sector, in line with the commitments made by the country in the Minamata Convention on Mercury, which aims to protect health and the environment.

Colombia_Justification:
Colombia promotes mercury-free technologies primarily through specific projects and international cooperation, but lacks a comprehensive, institutionalized national program. The country participates in the UNDP's Planet GOLD project, which promotes mercury-free technologies [19].
Law 1658 of 2013 prohibits the use of mercury in mining, which indirectly promotes alternative technologies, but does not establish specific incentives for their adoption [20].

Peru_Justification: Yes, the Peruvian government promotes the adoption of safer, mercury-free technologies, but it does so primarily through public and cooperative programs and projects (training, provision of gravimetric plants, technical assistance, and regulatory frameworks), rather than through a permanent government subsidy or a specific tax regime for these technologies. The National Implementation Plan for the Minamata Convention (Supreme Decree No. 004-2019-MINAM) mandates that public institutions implement measures to reduce and eliminate mercury use, including the promotion of alternative technologies. At the operational level, the planetGOLD Peru project (GEF/UNDP and national partners) was the main vehicle for introducing and disseminating mercury-free technologies: training, technical guides, and the provision and commissioning of gravimetric plants. The Ministry of Energy and Mines (MINEM) has signed technical cooperation agreements (Artisanal Gold Council) and has participated in the provision and promotion of clean technologies for artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), in coordination with the Ministry of the Environment (MINAM) and partners.

Ecuador_Justification:
Articles 35 and 37 of the Regulations for the Special Regime of Small-Scale Artisanal Mining provide for the development of processes to promote clean technologies to reduce emissions and waste; and, specifically, they provide for the adoption of procedures that avoid the use of mercury.

In this regard, the PlanetGOLD project promotes the transition to mercury-free technologies and reduces the use of mercury in mining activities, in accordance with the Minamata Convention. The project provides access to clean technologies, financing, and ongoing training.

Question: Are women, traditional communities and Indigenous peoples specifically included in sustainable ASM initiatives?
Bolivia_Justification: Mining regulations only mention Indigenous peoples, recognizing their rights and including them in prior consultation processes and participation in environmental management. However, there are no regulations or institutional public programs that include women and Indigenous people in sustainable initiatives; it is NGOs and projects that generate these types of initiatives. For example, gender is a cross-cutting issue in the PlanetGOLD project. The Indigenous Women of the Amazon Program, promoted by Conservation International, can also be mentioned.
Bolivia_Sources:
- PlanetGOLD Bolivia. https://www.planetgold.org/es/bolivia
- Conservación Internacional. Disponible en: https://www.conservation.org/bolivia/INICIATIVASDESTACADAS/women-fellowship
Brazil_Justification:
Research on Criterion 51, concerning the inclusion of women and indigenous peoples in sustainable artisanal and small-scale mining (ASSM) initiatives in Brazil, reveals a complex and often contradictory scenario. While some initiatives and milestones recognize the importance of these groups, the reality on the ground, especially regarding illegal mining, presents significant challenges.

The IRMA Standard for Responsible Mining [1] provides a framework that, in principle, supports the inclusion of women and indigenous peoples in ASSM. Specifically, Chapter 3.6, dedicated to Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining, highlights the involvement of men and women in ASSM activities [1, p. 292]. It emphasizes the need to engage diverse stakeholders, including informal operators and associations, and suggests that different approaches may be necessary for men and women.

However, the reality of illegal mining, especially in indigenous territories, is marked by violence, exploitation, and human rights violations. Reports indicate that the presence of illegal miners on indigenous lands leads to increased violence, including sexual violence against indigenous women and girls.
Brazil_Sources: [2] CIMI. (2022). Relatório de Violência Contra os Povos Indígenas no Brasil. Disponível em: https://cimi.org.br/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/relatorio-violencia-contra-os-povos-indigenas-no-brasil-2021-cimi.pdf
Colombia_Justification:
Women and Indigenous Peoples are partially included in some sustainable ASM initiatives, but not comprehensively in all national policies. The Better Gold Initiative includes specific criteria for gender equity and the participation of Indigenous communities [2].
Prior consultation with Indigenous Peoples is constitutionally established, but there are no specific programs that systematically integrate women and Indigenous Peoples into sustainable ASM initiatives at the national level.

3.Decreto 1320 de 1998
•URL: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=1392
•Regula consulta previa
Peru_Justification: Yes, women and Indigenous peoples are explicitly included in policies and plans; however, their effective benefit depends on implementation (resources, inter-institutional coordination, quality of consultation processes, and regional reach). The Ministry of Energy and Mines approved the National Gender Plan for Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining 2023–2025 (Ministerial Resolution No. 524-2023-MINEM/DM), which incorporates measures to promote the participation, training, and empowerment of women in small-scale and artisanal mining. Furthermore, the National Multisectoral Policy for Small-Scale and Artisanal Mining to 2030 (Supreme Decree No. 016-2022-EM) requires territorial and intersectoral approaches that consider local populations and coordination with competent authorities; in addition, the State must respect the right to prior consultation for measures that affect Indigenous peoples (Law No. 29785 / Convention 169). Nevertheless, the participation of these vulnerable groups remains a challenge.

Ecuador_Justification: There are no regulations specifically aimed at including gender and intercultural approaches in sustainable artisanal and small-scale farming (ASF) initiatives. However, Ecuador does have a law on the purple economy that promotes the inclusion of these approaches from a labor perspective, and the UNDP is promoting a project to this end.

Question: Are there governmental programs to collaborate with NGOs, academic institutions, or international agencies to promote sustainable ASM practices?
Bolivia_Justification: In addition to what has already been mentioned in the justification, the work of several NGOs, such as WCS Bolivia, can be highlighted. WCS Bolivia has worked on the following projects: “Capacity Building for Reducing the Impacts of Mining in the Protected Areas of Apolobamba, Madidi, and Pilón Lajas,” funded by CEPF; “Incorporating Best Practices in Mining Operations in the Madidi-Pilón Lajas-Cotapata Conservation Corridor in Bolivia,” also funded by CEPF; and “Creation of a Regional Strategy to Integrate Environmental and Social Safeguards into Mining Practices in the Tropical Andes Hotspot.” Helvetas funded the project “Implementation of Cleaner Production Measures in the Upper Suches River Basin.” These are just some of the many NGOs that have developed research and projects related to gold mining and artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM).

Brazil_Justification:
Yes, Brazil demonstrates clear government programs and active collaboration with NGOs, academic institutions, and international agencies to promote sustainable practices in artisanal and small-scale mining (ASGM), as evidenced by its partnerships with UNEP, participation in the Global Coalition for Action on ASGM, and the National Action Plan for Mercury-Free Gold Mining.

The Ministry of Mines and Energy (MME) is developing a partnership with UNEP for a small-scale gold mining project, aiming to promote safer and more sustainable practices in the sector. Brazil also participates in the Global Coalition for Action on Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining (ASGM), which seeks to transform the sector into a catalyst for sustainable development.

The National Action Plan for Mercury-Free Gold Mining, launched in November 2022, is a collaborative effort involving several Brazilian ministries, with financial support from the GEF and technical assistance from UNEP and the Foundation for Technological Development in Engineering (FDTE).

The country collaborates with USAID through the "Legal Gold" program, with the UNDP on the Planet Gold project, and with the Alliance for Responsible Mining (ARM) [21].

Question: Are there clear protection mechanisms for individuals working in areas of mining?
Bolivia_Justification: In addition to the General Law on Hygiene, Occupational Safety and Welfare, there is the Technical Safety Standard NTS 009/23 of the Ministry of Labor and Social Welfare. This technical standard establishes mandatory guidelines for the presentation and approval of the Occupational Safety and Health Management Program (PGSST). This document aims to prevent occupational risks, work accidents and occupational diseases, guaranteeing safe and healthy conditions for workers; however, there are no specific mechanisms for people who work in mining areas.

Brazil_Justification: Yes, there are protection mechanisms for mining workers. Brazilian labor law establishes occupational safety and health standards, which are monitored by the Ministry of Labor and Employment. Furthermore, inspection operations rescue workers from conditions analogous to slavery in illegal mining areas, demonstrating the State's role in protecting workers' rights.
