Question: Is there recognition or promotion of international certification schemes by the government?
Bolivia_Justification: The Cotapata Gold Mining Cooperative R.L. was the first cooperative in Bolivia to obtain Fairmined and Fairtrade certification, with the support of Cumbre del Sajama S.A. However, the Bolivian State has not established any international certification programs.
Bolivia_Sources: - ARM. Cooperativa Cotapata – la primera mina certificada con Fairtrade y Fairmined. Disponible en: https://www.responsiblemines.org/2010/06/cooperativa-cotapata-la-primera-mina-certificada-con-fairtrade-y-fairmined/#:~:text=COOPERATIVA%20COTAPATA%20%28Bolivia%29%20%E2%80%93%20la%20primera%20organizaci%C3%B3n%20de,del%20Cumbre%20del%20Sajama%20por%20este%20historico%20logro%21
Brazil_Justification:
The Brazilian government, particularly through the Ministry of Mines and Energy (MME) and the National Mining Agency (ANM), demonstrates interest in recognizing and promoting certifications for responsible mining practices, including those relevant to artisanal and small-scale gold mining (ASM). While there is no explicit and direct governmental endorsement of 'Fairmined' or 'CRAFT' certifications as official government schemes, there are clear indications of related governmental and institutional efforts to align with international standards and promote traceability and responsible practices in the ASM sector.

Key findings:

Recognition of Standards-Based Certifications (MME/ANM): The Brazilian government, through the MME, has an initiative entitled 'Recognition of Standards-Based Certifications'. This initiative, active from November 1, 2023 to January 22, 2024, aimed to establish guidelines for the use of accredited inspection of mining companies. The central idea is to encourage the creation of and adherence to certifications based on technical standards and models that promote better environmental, social, and governance (ESG) practices.

ANM Global Initiative for Small-Scale Mining: The National Mining Agency (ANM) is integrating a global initiative to transform small-scale mining. This initiative, supported by the World Gold Council, governments, companies, and civil society, aims to transform a sector marked by informality and violations into a lever for sustainable development. Brazil is considered a strategic actor in this process.

Partnership between the OCB System and ARM for the CRAFT Code: The OCB System (Organization of Brazilian Cooperatives) and the Alliance for Responsible Mining (ARM) have established a partnership to bring the OECD's international Due Diligence benchmark to Brazil, adapted to the reality of artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), through the implementation of the CRAFT Code. This partnership, formalized on June 27, 2023, aims to support Brazilian mining cooperatives in challenges related to traceability, management, and risk mitigation.

Fairmined and CRAFT as Fundamental Tools: An article by ApexBrasil (Brazilian Trade and Investment Promotion Agency) from May 14, 2025, highlights that tools such as Fairmined, CRAFT, and RJC certifications are fundamental to ensuring transparency and generating trust in responsible mining.

In short, although there is no direct governmental 'promotion' of Fairmined or CRAFT as official Brazilian certifications, the government and related institutions are actively engaged in creating frameworks and supporting initiatives that recognize and align with international standards such as CRAFT, and acknowledge the importance of certifications like Fairmined for responsible MAPE (Management of Endocrinology, Employment, and Tourism).
Brazil_Sources:
https://www.gov.br/participamaisbrasil/reconhecimento-de-certificacoes-baseadas-em-padroes
https://www.gov.br/anm/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/anm-integra-iniciativa-global-inedita-para-transformar-a-mineracao-em-pequena-escala

Colombia_Justification:
The Colombian government recognizes and actively promotes international certification schemes. The Ministry of Mines and Energy is the official counterpart of the Better Gold Initiative, which works with international standards such as Fairmined and CRAFT [2].
The Alliance for Responsible Mining (ARM), the organization behind the Fairmined standard, is a strategic partner of the BGI in Colombia. Furthermore, the CRAFT code has been piloted at artisanal gold mining sites in Colombia [4].

•Pilotaje de códigos internacionales en territorio nacional
Peru_Justification: The Peruvian government does not officially recognize or promote international certification schemes such as Fairmined or CRAFT in its regulations. Their promotion in the country comes primarily from NGOs, international cooperation agencies, and the market itself.

Ecuador_Justification: There is no government recognition or promotion of international certification schemes associated with responsible mining or fair trade. Currently, the only requirements for international refiners of non-monetary gold certification are proof of implementation of environmental management system standards.

Question: Is there a government incentive for the adoption of gold certification?
Bolivia_Justification: The Regulations to Law No. 1503 have implicitly established incentives; however, these are not related to gold certifications. The incentives are related to domestic sales aimed at strengthening international gold reserves. Sellers are exempt from paying the Mining Royalty (Art. 10), and another incentive is the discount applied to the international price (Art. 6).
Bolivia_Sources:
- Resolución de Directorio Nº 096/2023 y Reglamento de Compra de Oro Destinado al Fortalecimiento de las Reservas Internacionales. Disponible en: https://www.bcb.gob.bo/webdocs/01_resoluciones/RD%20096%202023_0.pdf
- Bolivia Tributaria (2023). Regalías y premios: incentivos para cooperativas que vendan oro al Banco Central de Bolivia. Disponible en: https://bibliotecatributariabolivia.com/2023/07/18/regalias-y-premios-incentivos-para-cooperativas-que-vendan-oro-al-banco-central-de-bolivia/

Brazil_Justification:
Brazil does not have legally structured direct incentives, but it does provide technical and political support for initiatives that promote certification and traceability, such as the "Green Gold Seal" and the policies of the DDSM/MME. These efforts constitute indirect and structural incentives for responsible gold certification.

Brazil has been actively pursuing initiatives to promote gold certification, particularly for artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), as a way to combat illegality and foster sustainable practices. A significant initiative is the "Green Gold Seal" project, which focuses on the certification and traceability of gold produced in the Pan-Amazon region. This project involves Brazil and other member countries of the Amazon Cooperation Treaty Organization (ACTO).

The "Green Gold Seal" project is led by the Instituto Biotec Amazônia, which won a federal government tender to implement this initiative. The project aims to transform artisanal mining into legal and sustainable operations by providing a system for certified and traceable gold. The process involves microanalysis using X-ray fluorescence and other digital technologies to create a geochemical database for each gold-producing region.

From the perspective of government agencies, the Ministry of Mines and Energy (MME), through its Department of Sustainable Development in Mining (DDSM), plays a crucial role in strengthening public policies for sustainable mining. The DDSM's objectives include integrating mineral policy with environmental, social, and territorial policies, and focusing on incentives for social responsibility and corporate sustainability in the mining sector. Its work is aligned with the Minamata Convention on Mercury, aiming to reduce and eliminate the use of mercury in MAPE (Mining, Energy, and Preservation).

While direct financial incentives for individual miners to adopt certification are not explicitly detailed as generalized programs, government support for projects like the "Green Gold Seal" and its broader policy framework for sustainable mining indicate a structure of indirect incentives. The certification itself, by increasing market value and ensuring legality, acts as a significant economic incentive for producers.

Colombia_Justification:
In Colombia, there are no direct and specific government incentives aimed at promoting the adoption of responsible gold certifications. However, the country participates in international economic incentive schemes linked to certified and formalized production.

The main initiative currently in operation is the Better Gold Initiative (BGI), a public-private partnership between the Swiss Better Gold Association (SBGA) and the Swiss State Secretariat for Economic Affairs (SECO), with the Colombian Ministry of Mines and Energy acting as the official counterpart. Through this mechanism, formalized and certified miners receive a development premium of US$1.00 per gram of responsible gold, with 70% allocated to community social and environmental projects and 30% to strengthening the productivity of mining operations.

Question: Is ASM gold mining recognized in the country’s National Biodiversity Strategy and Action Plan (NBSAP)?
Bolivia_Justification: The Plurinational Strategic Policy for the Integrated and Sustainable Management of Biodiversity, Action Plan 2019-2030, establishes in Strategic Area 4, under the action line "Implementation of actions for the maintenance of biodiversity in the development of economic-productive activities and strategic projects," the goal for 2025 of developing a protocol for the proper handling of biodiversity in mining. No specific actions for artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) were found.

Brazil_Justification: Brazil's National Strategy and Action Plan for Biodiversity (NSBAP), under the Convention on Biological Diversity, does not explicitly recognize artisanal and small-scale gold mining (ASGM) or its specific impacts/management in detail. Although the NSBAP broadly addresses biodiversity conservation and mentions 'mining' as an activity with environmental impacts, a direct link to or specific recognition of ASGM gold mining within the NSBAP framework, as required by Criterion 53, was not found through keyword searches in the document.

Question: Does the country have specific programs, targets, or funding mechanisms for ecosystem restoration in areas affected by ASM gold mining?
Bolivia_Justification: Environmental and mining regulations do not establish restoration activities. Environmental regulations require the submission of closure and abandonment plans (Supreme Decree No. 3549), and the RAAM (Regulations for the Management of Mining Areas) in various articles (Articles 45, 46, paragraphs 16 and 18), 50, paragraph 5), 51, 53, paragraph 4), 67, 68, 69, and Chapter IV of Title VIII) establish the submission of a closure and rehabilitation plan for the area. However, there are no specific programs for ecosystem restoration.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Reglamento Ambiental para Actividades Mineras. Disponible en: https://sea.gob.bo/digesto/CompendioII/N/149_DS_24782.pdf
- D.S. Nº 3549. Disponible en: http://snia.mmaya.gob.bo/web/modulos/Biblioteca/items/03092021-010949-532781_209/03092021-010949-532781_209.pdf
Brazil_Justification:
Partially. Brazil has legal instruments and environmental programs of national scope that address the recovery of areas degraded by mining activities, including artisanal gold mining (MAPE). However, there is no specific federal program dedicated exclusively to the restoration of ecosystems degraded by MAPE, nor are there exclusive financing mechanisms for this purpose.

Brazilian legislation obliges entrepreneurs, including formalized artisanal miners, to recover areas degraded by mining, as stipulated in the Mining Code (Decree-Law No. 227/1967) – Article 47 and Decree No. 9.406/2018 (which regulates the Mining Code) – Article 98.

Law No. 12.651/2012 (New Forest Code) – Article 41: creates the Environmental Regularization Program (PRA) and mechanisms for the recovery of degraded Permanent Preservation Areas and Legal Reserves, also applicable to areas affected by mining.

Furthermore, the National Program for the Recovery of Native Vegetation (Proveg), created by Decree No. 8,972/2017, and the National Plan for the Recovery of Native Vegetation (Planaveg) aim to restore 12 million hectares by 2030, including areas degraded by mining, but without specifying the type of activity or source of degradation.

Axis 5.2 of Planaveg proposes to “support the implementation of restoration projects linked to large-scale compensation involving large financial volumes (originating from energy, mining, transportation, water, and sanitation projects)”.

Axis 8.3 of Planaveg proposes to “regulate mechanisms for the allocation and monitoring of a portion of the financial compensation resources from oil and natural gas exploration, mining, and energy generation activities for native vegetation restoration (RVN) projects, prioritizing public areas and socially and/or environmentally sensitive areas”.

These goals acknowledge mining as a driver of degradation and foresee the use of compensatory resources for ecosystem recovery, but do not directly address artisanal mining (MAPE).

Colombia has policies, programs, and financing mechanisms aimed at restoring degraded ecosystems, although there is no specific national program directed exclusively at areas affected by artisanal and small-scale gold mining (ASM).

The National Restoration Plan 2015–2030, led by the Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development (MinAmbiente), establishes general goals for ecological restoration, rehabilitation, and recovery of strategic ecosystems, including areas impacted by extractive activities. However, this instrument does not explicitly identify the areas degraded by ASM nor does it define specific funding lines for them.

The Regional Autonomous Corporations (CARs) implement regional ecological restoration programs financed with their own resources, environmental royalties, and international cooperation. These actions have included interventions in territories affected by gold mining, but they are carried out in a fragmented and decentralized manner, without a national coordinating framework.

The National Biodiversity Strategy mentions the Environmental and Social Remediation Program (PRAS), which aims to eliminate the environmental liabilities of extractive activities, including mining. The program includes the development of a National Information System for Comprehensive Remediation (SINARI) to inventory sources of pollution from activities such as mining. Question: Are there requirements or guidance for restoration, remediation, or compensation for degraded areas?
Bolivia_Justification:
Indeed, environmental and mining regulations establish general requirements for rehabilitation. The RAAM, in addition to closure and post-closure obligations, stipulates that an Audited Report must be prepared three years after closure (Art. 69), the content of which was approved by Administrative Resolution VMABCCGDF No. 048/20, dated October 22, 2022.

However, the regulations do not establish specific requirements for carrying out the restoration or rehabilitation of degraded areas.
Bolivia_Sources: - Resolución Administrativa VMABCCGDF Nº048/20 de fecha 22 ed octubre de 2022. Disponible en: https://es.scribd.com/document/900917441/Ra-Vmabccgdf-048-2020-Informe-Cierre-Auditado
Brazil_Justification:
Brazil has a comprehensive legal and regulatory framework that requires the restoration, remediation, and compensation of areas degraded by mining activities. These requirements are enshrined in the Federal Constitution (Article 225, Paragraph 2) and detailed in various decrees, laws, and normative instructions. The Degraded Area Recovery Plan (PRAD), established by Decree 97.632/89, is a crucial instrument that requires mining projects to present a detailed plan for environmental recovery, generally during the environmental licensing process with the Environmental Impact Study (EIA) and the Environmental Impact Report (RIMA).

The Mining Code Regulations (Decree No. 9.406, of June 12, 2018) further details the responsibilities of miners, including the prevention, mitigation, and compensation of environmental impacts, the preservation of workers' health and safety, the prevention of environmental disasters, and the environmental recovery of impacted areas. This includes mine closure and decommissioning of all facilities, such as tailings dams. Failure to comply with these obligations may lead to administrative, civil, and criminal liabilities, with sanctions described in Law 9.605/98 (Law on Environmental Crimes and Administrative Offenses) and Federal Decree 6.514/08, including fines and imprisonment.

Although the National Plan for the Recovery of Native Vegetation (Planaveg) and the National Program for the Recovery of Native Vegetation (Proveg) are not specific to artisanal mining (MAPE), both recognize mining activity as a driver of degradation and propose mechanisms for financial compensation and large-scale ecological restoration. Established by Decree No. 8,972/2017, Planaveg presents guidelines also applicable to the mineral sector:

Action 5.2 – Support the implementation of native vegetation recovery (RVN) projects linked to large-scale environmental compensation, including mining, energy, transportation, water, and sanitation projects.

Action 8.3 – Proposes the regulation of the allocation of part of the financial compensation resources from mining (CFEM) and other extractive sectors to ecological restoration projects and funds, prioritizing public and sensitive areas.

For artisanal and small-scale gold mining (MAPE), the Program to Support the Development of Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining (Pró-Mape), established by Decree No. 10,966/2022, aims to integrate and strengthen policies for sustainable development, encouraging best practices, formalization, and the promotion of health, assistance, and dignity in the communities involved.

Categories: Gold Supply Chain Regulation and Transparency
Question: Does the country require formal registration of all actors in the ASM gold supply chain (miners, traders, and exporters)?
Bolivia_Justification:
It should be clarified that, since mining is not characterized and therefore small-scale mining (ASM) is not defined in mining regulations, there is no specific formal registry for ASM.

However, Law No. 535, in Article 56, paragraph IV, states that SENARECOM will be responsible for registering and controlling the marketing of minerals and metals in the domestic market and international trade. Thus, SENARECOM is authorized to maintain the Mining Identification Number (NIM) registry, which is used to monitor mining operators and allows for the marketing of minerals, including gold (Article 87, paragraph b)). Furthermore, mineral traders must also have a registration, called the Withholding Agent Identification Number (NIAR) (Article 87, paragraph c)).

Ministerial Resolution No. 165/2017 regulates the scope and control of gold marketing from marginal deposits and small-scale mining. The same Resolution expands registration to include mineral trading companies, manufacturing companies, and jewelry stores.

In 2017, Supreme Decree No. 3405 was enacted, creating the Single Mining Registry (RUM) to provide legally recognized Mining Producers and Mining Operators, as defined by Law No. 535, with a unique identification code to expedite and facilitate various procedures within the state-owned mining and metallurgical sector. However, no evidence has been found that this RUM has been created or that a specific system exists.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Ley Nº 535 de Minería y Metalurgia. Disponible en: https://www.comibol.gob.bo/ images/noticias/Ley_535.pdf
- Resolución Ministerial Nº 165/2017 de fecha 16 de Agosto de 2017. Disponible en: https://nlc-compendio.com/files/ac31413.pdf
- Decreto Supremo Nº 3405 de fecha 29 de noviembre de 2017. Disponible en: https://faolex.fao.org/docs/pdf/bol172301.pdf
- SGBI. Gold Value Chains in Bolivia, Colombia and Peru: Incentives and Rationale around Formalisation. Disponible en: https://ororesponsable.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/Gold-Value-Chains-in-Bolivia-Colombia-and-Peru_-Incentives-and-Rationale-around-Formalisation-021224.pdf
Brazil_Justification: Yes, Brazil requires formal registration of the main actors in the MAPE gold supply chain. Artisanal miners must obtain a Mining Permit (PLG) from the National Mining Agency (ANM). Financial institutions and cooperatives that buy gold at the source must be authorized by the Central Bank and the ANM, and all transactions must be documented with invoices. A decision by the Supreme Federal Court (STF) in March 2025 eliminated the presumption of good faith, obligating all buyers, including exporters, to prove the legal origin of the gold, which reinforces the need for formal registrations throughout the chain.
Brazil_Sources:
PROJETO DE LEI Nº 3.025 DE 2023 - Planalto
Requerer Permissão de Lavra Garimpeira - Gov.br
LEI Nº 7.766, DE 11 DE MAIO DE 1989 - Planalto
STF invalida norma que estabelecia presunção de boa-fé no comércio de ouro - STF Notícias
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has legal mechanisms that regulate the formal registration of certain actors in the gold supply chain, but these do not comprehensively cover all levels of artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM).

Decree 276 of 2015 established the Single Registry of Mineral Traders (RUCOM), administered by the National Mining Agency (ANM). This registration is mandatory for all mineral traders and buyers, who must prove the legal origin of the gold through certificates issued by mining titleholders or sales authorizations from formally registered artisanal miners. RUCOM is a key tool for traceability and control of mineral trade.

However, registration is not universally required for all artisanal and small-scale (ASSM) miners, especially in the case of subsistence or artisanal mining, defined in Article 31 of Law 685 of 2001 (Mining Code) and regulated by Decree 1666 of 2016. This type of mining is exempt from some formal requirements, and artisanal miners can trade gold through simplified authorizations, without mandatory individual registration with the National Mining Agency (ANM).

Meanwhile, gold exporters must be formally registered with the National Directorate of Taxes and Customs (DIAN), comply with foreign exchange regulations, and present certificates of origin issued by the owners or traders registered in the Single Registry of Gold Exporters (RUCOM).

Peru_Justification: Yes, Peru requires the formal registration of all actors in the gold supply chain. In the case of Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining (ASM), producers must register in the Comprehensive Mining Formalization Registry (REINFO) for the duration of their formalization process, in accordance with Supreme Decree No. 1000-2016-2017. No. 018-2017-EM and Law No. 31007. Once this process is completed, they are transferred to the Registry of Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining Producers, regulated by Law No. 27651 and its regulations (Supreme Decree No. 013-2002-EM). In the case of medium and large miners, they do not enter the REINFO, but rather require a mining concession granted by INGEMMET or the Regional Directorates of Energy and Mines, according to the Consolidated Text of the General Mining Law (Supreme Decree No. 014-92-EM), in addition to having an approved EIA and the authorization to start operations granted by the MINEM. For their part, gold traders and processors must be registered in the Registry of Gold Traders and Processors (RECPO) created by Supreme Decree No. No. 012-2012-EM, a mandatory requirement for buying, selling, or stockpiling minerals of legal origin. Finally, gold exporters must be registered in the Single Taxpayer Registry (RUC) of SUNAT, complying with the provisions of the General Customs Law (Legislative Decree No. 1053), which requires proof of traceability and legal origin of gold for export.

Question: Are there specific permits or procedures required for exporting ASM gold?
Bolivia_Justification: The permits and requirements for gold exports are not exclusive to artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), as ASM is neither characterized nor identified in Bolivian mining regulations. Therefore, the requirements and permits apply to all artisanal and small-scale mining operators (APMs) that export gold. Supreme Decree No. 5076 aims to regulate gold exports, authorizing the Central Bank of Bolivia (BCB) to issue the Export Certificate (CEO), which serves as the supporting document for customs clearance of exports. This procedure also includes completing Form M-03 of the National Service for Customs and Information Technology (SENARECOM) through the National System for Customs and Information Technology (SINACOM) platform, as mentioned in the justification.

Brazil_Justification: Yes, although there are no direct export permits for MAPE (Mining Areas of the Amazon), the Brazilian legal system channels MAPE gold into the formal domestic market through authorized financial institutions before export. Law No. 7,766/89 requires that gold from mining be sold to institutions authorized by the Central Bank. The recent overturning of the "good faith" rule by the Supreme Federal Court and the implementation of electronic invoicing for gold have increased control over the origin of gold, ensuring that only legally acquired gold can be exported.
Brazil_Sources:

Exclusivo: fim da boa-fé no comércio de ouro reduz atividade garimpeira declarada na Amazônia - Repórter Brasil
Colombia_Justification:
In Colombia, gold exports are subject to general customs permits and procedures applicable to all actors in the mining sector, without distinction between gold from artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) and gold from medium- or large-scale operations.

Gold exporters must be registered with the National Directorate of Taxes and Customs (DIAN) as foreign trade users, comply with the exchange regulations of the Central Bank of Colombia (Banco de la República), and submit the documentation required by Decree 1165 of 2019 (customs regime), including export declarations, commercial invoices, and certificates of origin for the mineral.

The Single Registry of Mineral Traders (RUCOM), established by Decree 276 of 2015, requires that all mineral transactions be accompanied by a legal certificate of origin issued by the mining titleholder or authorized trader. This requirement does not differentiate between types of mining, so gold from formalized artisanal and small-scale mining or from artisanal panning is exported under the same procedures as that from large-scale mining.

Peru_Justification:
Yes. In Peru, the export of gold from Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining (ASM) is subject to specific permits and procedures that ensure its formality and traceability:
Producer Formality: The gold must come from miners registered in the Comprehensive Registry of Mining Formalization (REINFO), according to Supreme Decree No. 018-2017-EM (Articles 1 and 2), or from those who have completed the process and are listed in the Administrative Registries of Artisanal and Small-Scale Mining Producers, created by the Regulations of Law No. 27651 (Supreme Decree No. 013-2002-EM, Articles 4 and 11).
Authorized Marketing: Sales can only be made through individuals or legal entities registered in the Special Registry of Gold Traders and Processors (RECPO/RCMPM), in accordance with Supreme Decree No. 013-2002-EM. No. 027-2012-EM (Art. 4.1).
Export:
Exports are processed through the Customs Declaration of Goods (DAM) in the Single Window for Foreign Trade (VUCE).
Crime Prevention and Traceability:
Gold traders and exporters are considered obligated parties under Law No. 27693 (Financial Intelligence Unit - Peru, Art. 8), which requires them to prove the legal origin of the mineral.
Art. 9 of the same law imposes specific obligations on them: to identify and verify clients, maintain documentation, and report suspicious transactions to the Financial Intelligence Unit (UIF).

Ecuador_Justification:
Gold traded with the Central Bank of Ecuador (BCE) must be of legal origin. Therefore, all interested individuals or legal entities must be authorized as Economic Agents for Gold Trading, which requires meeting the following requirements:
Having registered mining rights with the relevant Ministry, currently the Ministry of Energy and Non-Renewable Natural Resources.
Being up-to-date with tax obligations as determined by the Internal Revenue Service (SRI); and not being registered as a defaulting supplier to the State according to information provided by the National Public Procurement Service.
Not being involved in money laundering or the financing of terrorism, nor in crimes sanctioned for the production or illicit trafficking of narcotic and psychotropic substances.
Registering and obtaining authorization as an Economic Agent with the Central Bank of Ecuador (BCE).

Question: Are files on ASM areas, including titles, licenses, embargoes, and monitored zones, available in open, updated formats compatible with geospatial analysis systems?
Bolivia_Justification:
It is evident that while the Mining Administrative Authority (AJAM) has a system that compiles information on mining areas, Special Administrative Entities (ATEs), mining contracts by grid and by ownership, reserves, and immobilized areas, its level of updating is unknown, as the information is not publicly accessible. Publication in the National Mining Gazette occurs when the AJAM orders the continuation of the Mining Administrative Contract (CAM) process and subsequently when the CAM is approved or rejected.

Although the Law of Administrative Procedures establishes in Article 18, paragraph I, that “individuals have the right to access archives, public records, and documents held by the Public Administration, as well as to obtain certificates or legalized copies of such documents, regardless of the form of expression—graphic, sound, image, or other—or the type of physical medium on which they appear,” the information at the AJAM is handled confidentially; that is, it is not easily accessible to the public.

On the other hand, the GEOBolivia portal has an atlas where general information on mining activities in Bolivia can be viewed; however, this would not be useful for the purposes of geospatial analysis and identification of MAPE.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Autoridad Jurisdiccional Administrativa Minera (AJAM). (2017). Reglamento de Registro Minero. Disponible en: https://www.autoridadminera.gob.bo/wp-content/uploads/2023/03/reglamento_registro_minero-1.pdf
- GeoBolivia. https://geo.gob.bo/apps/3181/embed#
- Ley Nº 2341 de Procedimientos Administrativos de fecha 23 de abril de 2002. Disponible en: https://www.dgac.gob.bo/wp-content/uploads/2019/08/2341.pdf
Brazil_Justification: Brazil offers open and up-to-date data on mining through SIGMINE and the ANM's Mining Registry, in formats compatible with geospatial systems. However, the explicit and easily accessible categorization of all MAPE areas, including embargoed and monitored zones, and their direct link to inspection and judicial data, is not yet fully clear and accessible to the public, justifying the partial score.
Brazil_Sources:
Sistema de Informações Geográficas da Mineração (SIGMINE)
Sistema de Cadastro Mineiro
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia makes some georeferenced information on the mining sector publicly available, but the files are not fully available in open formats or uniformly updated, and there is no specific identification of artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) areas within official systems.

The National Mining Registry, managed by the National Mining Agency (ANM), offers georeferenced information on current, pending, or expired mining titles, available through its online map viewer. However, this system does not classify or distinguish areas associated with ASM, nor does it include specific information layers on subsistence mining, artisanal panning, or formalization processes. Furthermore, much of the information is published in limited PDF or KML formats, which restricts its interoperability with advanced GIS (Geographic Information Systems) platforms.

The Colombian Mining Information System (SIMCO), for its part, provides statistics and reports on production, marketing, and licensing, but not in an open format or downloadable in editable geospatial layers (such as interoperable shapefiles, GeoJSON, or WMS).

Although Law 1712 of 2014 (Transparency and Access to Public Information Law) requires state entities to publish their data in open and reusable formats, the updating and geospatial standardization of mining information still presents significant gaps, especially regarding artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM) and areas under embargo or environmental monitoring measures.
Colombia_Sources:
Agencia Nacional de Minería (ANM) – Catastro Minero Nacional. Disponible en: https://www.anm.gov.co/

Sistema de Información Minero Colombiano (SIMCO). Disponible en: https://minenergia.gov.co/es/repositorio-normativo/normativa/sistema-de-informaci%C3%B3n-minera/

Peru_Justification:
Yes, INGEMMET maintains the national mining registry with updated geospatial information that is publicly accessible through GEOCATMIN. REINFO also provides updated information on mining formalization areas in formats compatible with geospatial analysis. SENACE, MINEM, and platforms such as GeoBosques (SERFOR) make relevant data available that complement the traceability and monitoring of these areas.

However, certain specific information, such as liens or encumbrances (SUNARP), or some environmental management instruments, is often published in closed formats (PDF or direct queries), which partially limits its automatic integration into geospatial analysis systems.

This geoportal is available in open format.

Question: Is there a strategy in place to ensure the traceability of gold from extraction to final sale?
Bolivia_Justification: Although the RUM (Unique Mining Registry) was established by regulation to provide a unique mining code to APMs (Mining Producers), no evidence of its implementation has been found, which could contribute to traceability. While APMs are registered through the NIM (Mining Identification Number), allowing them to sell their minerals, the primary traceability record would be through mineral sales, specifically the completion of Form M-02 and, in the case of exports, Form M-03, both issued by SENARECOM (National Mining and Metals Registry). SENARECOM, according to Article 87 of Law No. 535, is responsible for monitoring compliance with legal regulations governing the domestic and international trade of minerals and metals, maintaining the Mining Identification Number Registry, and other duties. However, since no supporting documentation is required during mineral trade, with the exception of the BCB (Central Bank of Bolivia), traceability mechanisms are lacking.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Ley Nº 535 de Minería y Metalurgia. Disponible en: https://www.comibol.gob.bo/ images/noticias/Ley_535.pdf
- SENARECOM. (n.d.). Requisitos para la Comercialización de Minerales. Disponible en: https://www.senarecom.gob.bo/requisitos-comercializacion-minerales.php
- SGBI. Gold Value Chains in Bolivia, Colombia and Peru: Incentives and Rationale around Formalisation. Disponible en: https://ororesponsable.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/Gold-Value-Chains-in-Bolivia-Colombia-and-Peru_-Incentives-and-Rationale-around-Formalisation-021224.pdf
Brazil_Justification: Brazil has traceability mechanisms, but they are still insufficient and have loopholes that allow illegal gold to enter the market. The Supreme Court's decision to end the presumption of good faith and the proposal of technologies such as DLT and molecular marking indicate an effort to improve the system, but full and effective implementation has not yet been achieved.
Brazil_Sources:
Instituto Escolhas. (2022). Sistema de Rastreio para o Ouro: Desafios e recomendações.
Brasil Mineral. (2025, Março 20). OURO | Decisão do STF acaba com a presunção da boa-fé na venda do ouro de garimpos.
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has regulatory instruments that establish the traceability of gold and other minerals, but it still lacks a comprehensive, fully operational, and nationwide system that allows tracking the mineral from extraction to final sale.

The Single Registry of Mineral Traders (RUCOM), created by Decree 276 of 2015, constitutes the first link in the traceability chain. Administered by the National Mining Agency (ANM), this registry requires gold traders and buyers to register and submit certificates of origin that prove the mineral's legality. However, RUCOM does not allow for continuous digital tracking of gold throughout the entire supply chain, limiting its traceability to the initial commercialization stage.
Colombia_Sources:
1.Decreto 276 de 2015 - RUCOM
•URL: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=61724
Peru_Justification: In Peru, several registries regulate actors in the gold supply chain, such as the Comprehensive Registry of Mining Formalization (REINFO) for artisanal and small-scale miners, the Special Registry of Gold Traders and Processors (RECPO/RCMPM) for those who acquire and process the mineral, and the Single Taxpayer Registry (RUC) of SUNAT (the Peruvian tax authority) for exporters. These instruments allow for the identification of authorized entities and verification that operations are conducted formally, but they do not, in themselves, constitute a traceability mechanism, as they do not allow for continuous tracking of the gold's journey from the mine to the market. A true traceability mechanism is currently in a pilot phase in Pataz (La Libertad region), using the digital application QORI, created by Emergency Decree No. 006-2025. This system requires traders to register each gold transaction, generating a history that tracks the mineral from its extraction to its final destination. Although it represents a significant step towards effective traceability, it has not yet been implemented nationwide and faces interoperability and control challenges. In conclusion, the Peruvian regulatory framework ensures formality through mandatory registrations, but the only real traceability mechanism is QORI, whose expansion and strengthening will be key to guaranteeing comprehensive gold tracking and reducing the risks of illegal trade.
Peru_Sources:
QORI: https://www.rumbominero.com/peru/noticias/mineria/qori-implementan-aplicativo-para-trazabilidad-obligatoria-de-oro-en-pataz/

Ecuador_Justification: There is no specific regulation that requires concrete traceability mechanisms.

Question: Is disclosure of transactions between miners, traders, and exporters mandatory?
Bolivia_Justification:
Mineral sales transactions must be registered using Form M-02, and in the case of exports, Form M-03 from SENARECOM.

However, the information provided by buyers (authorized trading companies) is only submitted to SENARECOM and is not subject to mandatory disclosure to the public.
Bolivia_Sources:
- SENARECOM, Compendio Normativo. Disponible en: https://www.senarecom.gob.bo/files/leyes/Compendio_Normativo_Minero_Metalurgico_SENARECOM.pdf
- SENARECOM, Formularios M-02 y M-03. Disponible en: https://www.senarecom.gob.bo/formularios.php
Brazil_Justification: Yes, the disclosure of transactions is mandatory in Brazil. The implementation of the Electronic Invoice for Gold (NF-e Ouro) in 2023 made the issuance of an electronic invoice mandatory for the first purchase of raw gold, for imports, exports, and domestic transactions. The Supreme Court's decision against the presumption of good faith further reinforces the need for verification and disclosure of the origin of gold.
Brazil_Sources:
Instrução Normativa RFB nº 2.138, de 29 de março de 2023
STF decide que procedência do ouro no Brasil não pode ser atestada pela palavra do vendedor
Colombia_Justification:
There is no specific obligation to publicly disclose transactions between miners, traders, and exporters in Colombia. Although the RUCOM (Single Registry of Traders) requires certificates of origin, it does not mandate the public disclosure of specific transactions [3].
Commercial transactions are considered private information, and there is no legal framework requiring their systematic public disclosure.
Colombia_Sources:
1.Decreto 276 de 2015
•URL: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=61724
•No establece divulgación pública de transacciones
Peru_Justification: In Peru, there is no general obligation to publicly disclose transactions between gold miners, traders, and exporters. What the regulations establish are registration and reporting mechanisms directed to the competent authorities, to which access is restricted. For example, traders and processors registered with the RECPO/RCMPM must keep a record of their operations and report periodically to the Ministry of Energy and Mines (MINEM), while exporters are required to declare their shipments through the Customs Declaration of Goods (DAM) and the Single Window for Foreign Trade (VUCE), under the supervision of the National Superintendency of Tax Administration (SUNAT) and the Customs Administration. Likewise, within the framework of money laundering prevention, Law No. 27693 considers traders and exporters of precious metals as obligated parties, imposing the duty to identify clients, maintain documentation, and report suspicious transactions to the Financial Intelligence Unit (UIF-Peru). More recently, the Emergency Decree... Decree No. 006-2025 has implemented a pilot system of mandatory digital traceability in Pataz through the QORI application, which allows for the real-time recording of transactions for auditing purposes. In conclusion, while transactions must be recorded and reported, this information is not publicly disclosed in open formats. The data remains under the control of state entities such as the Ministry of Energy and Mines (MINEM), the National Superintendency of Tax Administration (SUNAT), and the Financial Intelligence Unit (UIF), meaning that gold traceability is an administrative and fiscal process, rather than an exercise in public transparency.

Although due diligence requirements exist for financial institutions and the Central Bank, there is no mandatory system for disclosing transactions across the entire supply chain.

Question: Does the legal framework require financial institutions to verify the legal provenance of gold-related transactions?
Bolivia_Justification: The regulations for financial institutions establish due diligence in a general way, without specifying for the mining sector or for transactions related to gold, so there is no record of it.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Normas Servicios Financieros LIBRO III - TÍTULO X. Disponible en: https://bolivia.infoleyes.com/norma/4576/recopilaci%C3%B3n-de-normas-servicios-financieros-libro-iii-t%C3%ADtulo-x
- Debida Diligencia en el Cumplimiento de Obligaciones Comerciales en Bolivia. Disponible en: https://ecglegal.com.bo/blog/debida-diligencia-en-el-cumplimiento-de-obligaciones-comerciales-en-bolivia
- Ley N° 393 de Servicios Financieros. Disponible en: https://www.asfi.gob.bo/images/MARCO_NORMATIVO/LEY/LEY_393.pdf
- ASFI - Recopilación de Normas. Disponible en: https://www.asfi.gob.bo/index.php/marco-normativo/recopilacion-de-normas-para-servicios-financieros.html
- UIF Bolivia - Ley N° 1768. Disponible en: https://www.uif.gob.bo/uif/images/stories/docs/marco_legal/ley_1768.pdf
Brazil_Justification: Yes, financial institutions are required to conduct due diligence. The Supreme Federal Court's decision in March 2025, which eliminated the presumption of good faith, imposes the obligation to verify the legal origin of gold. Furthermore, the Money Laundering Law (Law No. 9.613/98) and regulations from the Central Bank and COAF (Council for Financial Activities Control) require financial institutions to implement anti-money laundering policies, including client identification and reporting of suspicious transactions.
Brazil_Sources:
STF invalida norma que estabelecia presunção de boa-fé no comércio de ouro - STF Notícias
Lei nº 9.613, de 3 de março de 1998
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia formally requires financial institutions and commercial entities involved in transactions related to gold and other precious metals to implement due diligence measures through mandatory anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing (AML/CFT) risk management regimes.

The Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing Risk Management System (SARLAFT), overseen by the Financial Superintendency of Colombia, and the Comprehensive AML/CFT Self-Control and Risk Management System (SAGRILAFT), established by the Superintendency of Companies, require companies in the real sector, including those engaged in the trade, refining, or export of precious metals, to implement due diligence procedures, customer identification, beneficial ownership identification, and suspicious transaction reporting.

External Circular 100-000016 of 2020 from the Superintendency of Companies updated the SAGRILAFT regulations, establishing a risk-based approach that requires commercial companies, branches of foreign companies, and sole proprietorships to periodically assess the risk associated with transactions involving precious metals and stones, as well as the obligation to maintain verifiable records and reports to the Financial Information and Analysis Unit (UIAF).

The UIAF, attached to the Ministry of Finance and Public Credit, is the agency responsible for centralizing, analyzing, and cross-referencing financial and commercial information from obligated entities, including gold trading, export, and financing operations. Its work allows for the detection of irregularities and the prevention of the use of the gold sector for illicit activities.

Additionally, Law 2177 of 2021 (Mining Banking Law) promotes the formal financial inclusion of mining actors, while reinforcing transparency and regulatory compliance obligations, ensuring that access to the banking system is conditional upon compliance with the due diligence and traceability requirements established by the competent authorities.
Colombia_Sources:
Circular Externa 100-000016 de 2020 (Superintendencia de Sociedades). Disponible en: https://www.supersociedades.gov.co/nuestra-entidad/normatividad/Paginas/circulares-externas.aspx

Peru_Justification: In Peru, financial institutions are required to apply due diligence measures to gold-related transactions, and good faith regarding the origin of the gold is not automatically presumed. Law No. 27693, which created the Financial Intelligence Unit (UIF-Peru), includes financial institutions and precious metal traders and exporters as obligated parties, imposing on them the responsibility to identify their clients, verify information, maintain transaction records, and report any suspicious transactions. Additionally, the Superintendency of Banking, Insurance, and Private Pension Fund Administrators (SBS), through Resolution No. 2660-2015, requires supervised entities to implement Know Your Customer (KYC) policies and enhanced due diligence in transactions considered high-risk, including those related to gold trading. In practice, this means that banks must evaluate and document gold-related transactions and immediately report any indication of irregularity to the Financial Intelligence Unit (UIF). In conclusion, Peruvian regulations establish that the financial traceability of gold depends on compliance with these control and verification mechanisms, which strengthens the transparency of operations and reduces the risk of illegally sourced gold entering the formal financial system.

Ecuador_Justification: Financial institutions are involved in the due diligence process. The Central Bank of Ecuador conducts an initial due diligence review based on its internal compliance procedures for linking mining activities. Requirements for economic agents include a compliance certificate from the Financial and Economic Analysis Unit (UAFE).

Question: Is there a centralized digital/electronic registry for gold supply chain transactions accessible to regulatory authorities and civil society?
Bolivia_Justification: SENARECOM has the SINACOM system platform, in which forms M-02 (internal sales) and M-03 (mineral export) are reported; however, this system is not freely accessible, meaning the information is not accessible to the general public.
Bolivia_Sources:
- SENARECOM – SINACOM. Disponible en: https://servicios2.sinacom.gob.bo
- SENARECOM - Portal oficial: https://www.senarecom.gob.bo/
- Ministerio de Minería y Metalurgia: https://mineria.gob.bo/
Brazil_Justification: Brazil is developing a centralized digital registration system, but it is not yet fully implemented and accessible. Bill PL 3025/2023 proposes the creation of SIG-Ouro, an electronic system to monitor and record all gold transactions. Currently, information is scattered across different systems, such as those of the Federal Revenue Service (NF-e) and the ANM (National Mining Agency), with limited access for civil society.
Brazil_Sources:
PROJETO DE LEI Nº 3.025 DE 2023 - Planalto
Instituto Escolhas. (2022). Sistema de Rastreio para o Ouro: Desafios e recomendações.
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia has made progress in creating a centralized digital registry for mineral transactions, including gold, through the Mineral Traceability Platform, established by Decree 2234 of 2023. This system is the main technological mechanism for registering, verifying, and controlling the extraction, transportation, processing, and marketing of minerals, with the aim of strengthening transparency, preventing money laundering, and ensuring traceability throughout the entire production chain.

The National Mining Agency (ANM) is the entity responsible for managing and integrating the information within the platform, as well as verifying production volumes, validating the identities of operators, controlling the quantities traded, and ensuring interoperability with other government institutions, such as the National Directorate of Taxes and Customs (DIAN), the Financial Information and Analysis Unit (UIAF), and the Ministry of Mines and Energy (MinEnergía).

Resolution 759 of 2024 from the National Mining Agency (ANM) regulated the technical operation of the platform, defining the procedures for electronic registration of mining transactions and the mandatory reporting mechanisms for actors in the supply chain. However, access to information is primarily restricted to regulatory authorities, without open access mechanisms or public consultation for civil society or the academic sector.

Consequently, although the country has a centralized digital system in the implementation phase, its use and coverage are still limited, and access to data is restricted to the relevant state institutions. Therefore, the score of 0.5 reflects partial compliance with the criterion: the legal and technological framework exists, but not full operational capacity or open access to information.
Colombia_Sources:
Decreto 2234 de 2023 (por el cual se establece la Plataforma de Trazabilidad de Minerales). Disponible en: https://www.funcionpublica.gov.co/eva/gestornormativo/norma.php?i=227210

Resolución 759 de 2024 (Agencia Nacional de Minería – reglamenta la Plataforma de Trazabilidad de Minerales). Disponible en: https://www.anm.gov.co/sites/default/files/2024-10-31-RESOLUCION-NUMERO-759-28-OCT-2024.pdf

Agencia Nacional de Minería (ANM) – Funciones del Sistema de Trazabilidad. Disponible en: https://www.anm.gov.co/
Peru_Justification: Partial registration exists through the RECPO and the QORI application, but there is no comprehensive, centralized digital record of all transactions in the gold supply chain. Information is available to regulatory authorities, but access for civil society is limited due to confidentiality concerns.

Question: Are there protocols for information sharing and joint enforcement with neighboring countries?
Bolivia_Justification: Bolivia is a member of the Amazon Cooperation Treaty Organization (ACTO), whose Treaty establishes the exchange of information among ACTO member countries. The Bogotá Declaration, issued in August 2025 during the Fifth Meeting of Presidents of the States Parties to the Treaty of Amazon Cooperation, reaffirms support for the Special Commission on Public Security and Transnational and Cross-Border Crimes in the Amazon Region (resolution RES/XIV/MRE-ACTO/09), to promote police, judicial, and intelligence cooperation in the fight against transnational illicit activities in the Amazon region, including information exchange, among other important aspects.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Aduana Nacional - Acuerdo Bolivia-Chile. Disponible en: https://www.aduana.gob.bo/aduana7/content/bolivia-y-chile-acuerdan-procedimientos-protocolos-e-intercambio-de-informaci%C3%B3n-en-el-%C3%A1mbito
- Comunidad Andina - I Informe del Observatorio Andino del Mercurio. Disponible en: https://www.comunidadandina.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/I-Informe-Observatorio-Andino-del-Mercurio.pdf
- COMIBOL - Foro Minero Chile Bolivia. Disponible en: https://www.comibol.gob.bo/index.php/ultimas-noticias/629-bolivia-y-chile-intercambian-conocimientos-sobre-mineria
- OTCA Tratado de Cooperación Amazónica. Disponible en: https://otca.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/01/TRATADO-DE-COOPERACI%C3%93N-AMAZ%C3%93NICA.pdf
- Declaración de Bogota (2025). Disponible en: https://www.cancilleria.gov.co/newsroom/news/declaracion-bogota-termino-v-encuentro-presidentes-organizacion-tratado-cooperacion
Brazil_Justification: Yes, Brazil has protocols for the exchange of information and joint law enforcement with neighboring countries. The Federal Police and the Armed Forces conduct joint operations with neighboring countries to combat cross-border crimes, including gold smuggling and illegal mining. The AURUM project also promotes the exchange of information and best practices among institutions in Amazonian countries.
Brazil_Sources:
Polícia Federal - Cooperação Internacional
AURUM - Prevenção da Mineração Ilegal do Ouro na Amazônia
Colombia_Justification:
Yes. Colombia has established protocols, bilateral agreements, and multilateral mechanisms for information exchange and joint law enforcement regarding environmental control and the fight against illegal mining, particularly in border areas of the Amazon.

At the bilateral level, Colombia maintains cooperation agreements with Brazil, Peru, Ecuador, and Venezuela focused on controlling illegal mining, environmental protection, and the shared management of transboundary natural resources.

At the multilateral level, Colombia actively participates in:

The Amazon Cooperation Treaty Organization (ACTO), which promotes the regional exchange of information on illegal mining, mercury trafficking, and deforestation through the Amazon Regional Observatory (ORA). This mechanism facilitates the exchange of satellite data, early warnings, and joint action protocols among member countries.

The Andean Committee against Illegal Mining (CAMI), created by the Andean Community of Nations (CAN), of which Colombia is a member state along with Bolivia, Ecuador, and Peru. The CAMI promotes the coordination of public policies, regulatory harmonization, and technical cooperation to combat illegal mining.

This includes the exchange of information on illicit mercury trafficking routes and financial flows, as well as coordinated border enforcement actions.

Joint Operations Artemisa, led by the Colombian armed forces with support from Brazil, Peru, and Ecuador.

These joint operations aim to control deforestation, illegal mining, and other illicit economies in the Amazon.

They incorporate protocols for direct communication between environmental, customs, and police authorities and have been replicated as a model for regional cooperation.

Additionally, the SINCHI Amazonian Institute for Scientific Research, under the coordination of the Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development, participates in regional environmental monitoring platforms and scientific information exchange with counterpart institutions in Brazil, Peru, and Ecuador, facilitating the early detection of transboundary environmental impacts related to gold mining.

Together, these bilateral and multilateral mechanisms consolidate a regional network of police, environmental and scientific cooperation that strengthens the capacity for joint response to illegal mining and associated crimes, ensuring the effective implementation of the law in border areas.
Colombia_Sources:
1.Operaciones Conjuntas Artemisa
•Operaciones coordinadas entre Colombia, Brasil, Perú y Ecuador
•Objetivo: Control de deforestación y minería ilegal

• URL: https://www.sinchi.org.co

However, these protocols are not specific to artisanal and small-scale mining (ASM), and their effectiveness in practice is limited due to differences in national legal frameworks.

Question: Do public security bodies have specific strategies in place for safeguarding Indigenous and traditional communities against crimes related to mining in their territories?
Bolivia_Justification: It is evident that public security forces do not have strategies for the protection of indigenous communities against mining-related crimes, although there is legislation such as the CPE where the rights of indigenous peoples are recognized, but these groups are vulnerable because they do not have public security forces.

- Defensoría del Pueblo - Informe Defensorial Madidi: https://www.defensoria.gob.bo/uploads/files/informe-defensorial-nuestra-casa-sin-derechos-vulneracion-del-ejercicio-de-normas-y-procedimientos-de-las-npioc-para-la-gestion-compartida-del-area-protegida-madidi-frente-a-la-mineria.pdf
- Ministerio de Gobierno: https://www.mingobierno.gob.bo/
Brazil_Justification: Yes, Brazil's public security forces, in conjunction with other government agencies, have specific strategies for protecting indigenous communities. The Kayapó Indigenous Land De-intrusion Operation (OD-TIKAY) and actions in the Yanomami Indigenous Land are examples of large-scale operations aimed at dismantling illegal mining and protecting indigenous territories and populations. These operations involve the Federal Police, the Armed Forces, FUNAI (National Indian Foundation), and IBAMA (Brazilian Institute of Environment and Renewable Natural Resources), among others.

Operação federal impõe prejuízo de R$ 79 milhões ao garimpo ilegal na Terra Indígena Kayapó — Ministério dos Povos Indígenas
Com mais de 3 mil operações em 2024, Governo Federal impõe prejuízo de R$ 267 milhões ao garimpo na Terra Indígena Yanomami
Colombia_Justification: Colombia has specific strategies in place to protect Indigenous communities from mining-related crimes. The National Police has a Group for the Protection of Ethnic Communities, and the National Army conducts specific operations in Indigenous territories affected by illegal mining.

Peru_Justification: There are some strategies and legal frameworks for the protection of Indigenous communities, such as Law No. 28736, which establishes special protection for peoples living in isolation. However, practical implementation is deficient, and Indigenous communities continue to face threats, violence, and criminalization when they defend their territories against illegal mining.
Peru_Sources: Ley N° 28736: https://spij.minjus.gob.pe/spij-ext-web/#/detallenorma/H917090
Ecuador_Justification: Currently, there is no specific strategy in place. However, in July 2025, the Organic Law for the Strengthening of Protected Areas came into effect. Article 6 of this law stipulates that the National Police, the Armed Forces, and the National Environmental Authority, within their respective areas of competence, will develop a coordinated Comprehensive Security Plan for the National System of Protected Areas, as well as specific intervention protocols. This plan must be updated periodically, at least annually. The Comprehensive Security Plan and the protocols must guarantee strict compliance with Article 57, paragraph 20, of the Constitution of the Republic of Ecuador, which limits military activities in these territories. This is without prejudice to the intervention of security forces in the event of the presence of organized crime or organized armed groups in protected areas, in order to protect the sovereignty and population of these areas.
Ecuador_Sources: Ley Orgánica para el fortalecimiento de las Áreas Protegidas.

Bolivia_Justification: For environmental crimes, POFOMA can be mentioned; however, there are no specialized units for illegal mining. According to AJAM regulations, specifically the Internal Regulations Against the Illegal Exploitation of Mineral Resources approved in 2018, police personnel accompany on-site inspections. Regarding the Public Prosecutor's Office, it is unclear whether they have specialized units; however, it has been shown that they are providing formal training to their personnel on illegal mining and strengthening investigative mechanisms.

Question: Does the country have bilateral or multilateral protocols or agreements in place to coordinate joint police operations for the enforcement of environmental and mining laws?
Bolivia_Justification: Bolivia cooperates with its neighbors in the fight against illegal mining through bilateral agreements and its participation in regional organizations. It is a member of the Amazon Cooperation Treaty and the Andean Community (CAN), which adopted Decision 774 (2012) on combating illegal mining, promoting joint actions, border control, and information sharing. In 2018, Bolivia and Peru signed the Cobija Protocol, which establishes the coordination of simultaneous operations to eradicate illegal mining in border areas such as Madre de Dios and Pando. At the institutional level, Bolivia has the Binational Border Commission (COMBIFRON) with Peru and participates in the CAN's Ad Hoc Commission on Illegal Mining, in addition to collaborating with INTERPOL and UNODC in tracing the illegal trade in mercury.
Bolivia_Sources: Protocolo de Cobija (2018); Decisión 774 de la CAN; OTCA (Declaración de Belém, 2023); INTERPOL (Programa LEAP, 2025).
Brazil_Justification: Brazil actively participates in regional agreements and initiatives aimed at combating illegal mining and environmental crimes in the Amazon. It is a founding member of the Amazon Cooperation Treaty (1978), which provides for joint actions among Amazonian countries to preserve the environment and control cross-border illicit activities. It also maintains bilateral agreements with neighbors, such as the Brazil-Colombia security agreement, which includes joint operations on the Amazonian border against illegal mining and deforestation, and the Brazil-Peru action plan (2009) for sustainable development and combating environmental crimes in the Acre-Madre de Dios-Pando region.
Brazil_Sources: https://news.mongabay.com/2025/08/cross-border-operation-cracks-down-on-environmental-crimes-in-the-amazon/#:~:text=Police%20seized%20more%20than%20310,bulldozers%2C%20trucks%2C%20crushers%20and%20other
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia actively participates in bilateral and multilateral agreements to combat illegal mining and environmental crimes in the Amazon. It is a party to the Amazon Cooperation Treaty (1978) and the Leticia Pact (2019), through which it committed to coordinating actions with Brazil, Peru, Ecuador, and Bolivia to combat deforestation and illegal mining. Within the framework of the Andean Community (Decision 774, 2012), Colombia collaborates with its neighbors on joint operations, information sharing, and the seizure of machinery and materials used in illicit mining activities.

There are also binational commissions with Peru, Ecuador, and Brazil that address security and environmental issues. For example, in 2023, a binational operation was carried out with Brazil on the Puré River, where 19 illegal mining dredges that were contaminating the water with mercury were destroyed. With Peru, Colombia participates in binational cabinets focused on controlling illegal mining along the Putumayo River.

Colombia hosted the Amazon Summit of the ACTO (Bogotá, 2025), where countries agreed to strengthen police and judicial cooperation and create a Special Commission for Amazonian Security to combat transboundary environmental crimes.
Colombia_Sources: OTCA (Declaración de Bogotá, 2025); Policía Nacional de Colombia (Operación Puré, 2023); INTERPOL (Green Shield, 2025); Comunidad Andina (Decisión 774, 2012).
Peru_Justification: Peru has a broad network of bilateral and multilateral agreements to combat illegal mining and environmental crimes. It is a signatory to the Amazon Cooperation Treaty and an active member of the Andean Community, within which framework it participates in the Andean Policy against Illegal Mining (Decision 774, 2012). At the bilateral level, it maintains the Cobija Protocol (2018) with Bolivia to conduct joint mining interdiction operations in border areas, as well as agreements with Brazil (Action Plan 2009) and Colombia (Binational Cabinets) that include cooperation on environmental security. Institutionally, Peru's Commissioner for the Formalization and Oversight of Illegal Mining maintains direct coordination with its Bolivian and Brazilian counterparts. The Environmental Prosecutor's Office, the National Police, and the Customs Superintendency collaborate with their counterparts in neighboring countries through information exchanges and joint operations.
Peru_Sources: Protocolo de Cobija (2018); Decisión 774 de la CAN; OTCA (Declaración de Bogotá, 2025); INTERPOL (Green Shield, 2025).
Ecuador_Justification: Ecuador participates in various regional cooperation frameworks for the control of illegal mining and environmental protection. It is a party to the Amazon Cooperation Treaty and the Andean Community, complying with Decision 774 (2012) on combating illegal mining. At the bilateral level, it maintains the Binational Commission for the Integrated Management of Border Basins with Peru, created under the 1998 peace treaty, which coordinates actions in the Chinchipe and Santiago-Cenepa river basins to combat illegal mining. With Colombia, Ecuador has been developing joint military and police surveillance plans with the Putumayo border since 2011, where binational illicit mining operations are taking place.
Ecuador_Sources: Tratado de Cooperación Amazónica (1978); CAN (Decisión 774, 2012); OTCA (Declaración de Bogotá, 2025); INTERPOL (Green Shield, 2025).

Question: Have courts issued binding decisions related to illegal gold mining or its socio-environmental impacts in the last five years?
Bolivia_Justification: There is Constitutional Resolution No. 05/2023, which CPILAP won in the Rurrenabaque court, regarding illegal activities in the Beni and Kaka rivers, among others, related to illegal mining. Likewise, there are Judgments No. 004/2023/AP and Plurinational Constitutional Judgment No. 1326/2023, which uphold the rejection of mining and the flawed prior consultation process by the communities and agricultural cooperatives of the municipalities of Palos Blancos and Alto Beni.
Bolivia_Sources:

Brazil_Justification: Yes, Brazilian courts have issued important binding decisions in the last five years. The Supreme Court's decision in March 2025, which ended the presumption of good faith in the gold trade, is an example of a decision with an impact on the entire production chain. Furthermore, the Federal Courts have issued preliminary injunctions to halt illegal mining activities and hold offenders accountable, as in the case of the Japurá River in Amazonas.

Peru_Justification:
In Peru, several binding decisions related to illegal gold mining and its socio-environmental impacts have been issued in recent years.

In 2025, the Constitutional Court issued a ruling clarifying that miners registered in the Comprehensive Registry of Mining Formalization (REINFO) cannot use this registration as a shield against criminal proceedings for environmental crimes. This decision had a significant impact, as it reinforced the idea that formalization does not equate to impunity for socio-environmental damage.

A landmark case in which the Public Prosecutor's Office acted against the socio-environmental impacts of illegal gold mining occurred in April 2023 in the Puno region. The Specialized Environmental Prosecutor's Office obtained a conviction against Adrián Víctor Mamani Mamani, who was illegally extracting gold in the district of Ananea, province of San Antonio de Putina. The investigations confirmed that the accused was operating without authorization, using heavy machinery, chutes, and motors that caused pollution in the Ramis River basin, as well as the destruction of grasslands and wetlands in the high Andean prairie. The Judiciary handed down a suspended sentence of three years and ten months in prison for the crimes of pollution and environmental damage.

Ecuador_Justification: In the last five years, the Criminal Chamber of the National Court of Justice has issued five convictions for the crime of illegal mining activity. This crime is defined in Article 260 of the Comprehensive Organic Criminal Code. All cases involved the unauthorized transport of mineral resources and resulted from roadside checkpoints. These are cases that could be considered minor. To date, no high-profile case has been decided by the National Court of Justice, the highest criminal court. According to press reports, there are many more cases that have been adjudicated in lower courts and many more that are currently being prosecuted. However, the cases adjudicated by the National Court have been used as an indicator, as it is the highest court in this matter.

Question: Have judicial decisions or jurisprudence recognizing natural ecosystems as subjects of rights generated legal restrictions, obligations, or the imposition of sanctions in legal cases related to mining activities?
Bolivia_Justification: Law No. 300 in Article 10, paragraph 7), establishes responsibilities and sanctions for those who violate their rights, especially the right to clean air and to live free from pollution.
Bolivia_Sources: - Ley Nº 300 Ley Marco de la Madre Tierra y Desarrollo Integral para Vivir Bien. Disponible en: https://sea.gob.bo/digesto/CompendioII/N/141_L_300.pdf
Brazil_Justification: No, Brazilian jurisprudence does not yet recognize natural ecosystems as subjects of law in a way that generates direct legal restrictions in mining cases. Although the debate on "Rights of Nature" exists in academia and in some specific legal initiatives, there is no body of consolidated judicial decisions that apply this concept to impose sanctions or obligations on mining activities.
Brazil_Sources: A natureza como sujeito de direitos - ((o))eco
Colombia_Justification: Colombia has pioneering jurisprudence that recognizes ecosystems as subjects of law, generating specific restrictions and obligations for mining activities. Ruling T-622/16 regarding the Atrato River and subsequent decisions have established important precedents.
Colombia_Sources:

•Establece obligaciones de protección contra deforestación y minería ilegal

•Múltiples decisiones de tutela han aplicado estos precedentes
•Restricciones específicas a actividades mineras en ecosistemas protegidos
Peru_Justification: Judicial and regulatory decisions recognizing ecosystems as subjects of rights have begun to pave a new path in socio-environmental protection against extractive activities. While a consolidated framework is not yet in place, some rulings and ordinances have set important precedents. The most emblematic case is that of the Marañón River, declared a subject of rights by the Nauta Court in 2024, which ordered the State and Petroperú to implement immediate actions to stop pollution from oil spills. At the local level, ordinances have been approved in Puno recognizing rivers, lakes, and springs as rights holders, although with limited effects. Furthermore, in 2025, a lawsuit was filed to grant Lake Titicaca the same protection, a process that is still underway. These advances show that, although important steps have been taken, the judicial recognition of nature in Peru remains incipient and in the process of consolidation.
Peru_Sources:
Río Marañon: https://www.pucp.edu.pe/climadecambios/noticias/sentencia-emblematica-el-rio-maranon-es-declarado-titular-de-derecho/?utm_source=chatgpt.com
Lago Titicaca: https://www.business-humanrights.org/en/latest-news/per%C3%BA-presentan-demanda-para-que-se-ordene-la-implementaci%C3%B3n-de-medidas-que-reconozcan-al-lago-titicaca-como-sujeto-de-derechos/?utm_source=chatgpt.com
Ecuador_Justification: In Ecuador, nature is considered to have three rights: to exist, to persist (through life cycles), and to be restored. Article 73 of the Constitution mandates the State to adopt restrictive and precautionary measures to prevent the extinction of species or the destruction of ecosystems; these are measures that apply in complex situations. In accordance with this constitutional provision, the Constitutional Court decided the case known as Los Cedros Protected Forest (2021). In this case, the State granted authorization for exploratory mining activity beneath this forest, which harbors fragile ecosystems and hundreds of endangered species of flora and fauna. The State, through the national environmental authority, granted environmental authorization because the project did not intersect with protected areas (where mining is prohibited by Article 407 of the Constitution) and because the project complied with current environmental regulations. However, the environmental authorization was challenged by the mayor of a municipality in the area before the Constitutional Court. After appeals, the case reached the Constitutional Court, the highest court of constitutional justice. There, the analysis focused on constitutional aspects, determining that the environmental authority failed to consider the constitutional obligation to prevent species extinction through the adoption of restrictive and precautionary measures. Therefore, it upheld the lower court's decision to revoke the environmental permits for violating the rights of nature.

Constitución de la República del Ecuador.

Question: Can individuals or communities file collective environmental actions or constitutional remedies in mining-related cases?
Bolivia_Justification: The CPE (Articles 135 and 136) establishes the mechanism of popular action, through which individuals or groups can file complaints. Likewise, constitutional remedies can be presented before the Plurinational Constitutional Court, as exemplified by Plurinational Constitutional Ruling 1326/2023-S1 of December 20, 2023, filed by the Indigenous and Native Peasant Nations and Peoples, as well as the agricultural cooperatives of the municipality of Palos Blancos, regarding the failure of the AJAM (Administrative Board of the Municipal Administration of the Municipalities of Michoacán) to comply with the prior consultation procedure.
Bolivia_Sources:
- Sentencia Constitucional Plurinacional 1326/2023-S1 de fecha 20 de diciembre de 2023.
- Constitución Política del Estado. Disponible en: https://diputados.gob.bo/wp-content/uploads/2022/02/cpe.pdf
Brazil_Justification:
Yes, Brazilian law allows individuals and communities to file collective environmental actions and constitutional appeals. The Public Civil Action (ACP) is a widely used instrument for defending the environment and the rights of communities affected by mining. The ACP can be filed to prevent, correct, or repair environmental damage, and can be brought by the Public Prosecutor's Office (Federal or State), Public Defender's Office, the Union, States, Municipalities and Autonomous Agencies, Foundations and public companies, and civil associations and NGOs legally constituted for at least one and a half years.

Through the Popular Action (Law No. 4717/1965), any citizen can file a lawsuit on behalf of the community, aiming to protect public and social assets, including the environment. This action can be brought against acts or omissions of public authorities or entities that violate the Federal Constitution, the law, or the principles of public administration, causing harm to the collective interest. It can be used, for example, to challenge environmental licenses granted irregularly for mining projects.

Another constitutional remedy that can be used is the Collective Writ of Mandamus (Law No. 12.016/2009), for the protection of a clear and certain right threatened by an illegal act or abuse of authority. It can be filed by political parties, professional associations, unions, and civil associations. It can be applied, for example, in cases where the State fails to provide environmental information or issues licenses without prior public consultation.
Brazil_Sources:
Mineração ilegal de ouro na bacia amazônica: dos problemas e desafios identificados à ação coletiva - Instituto Igarapé
Acordo de R$ 170 bilhões para reparação de desastre de Mariana é homologado pela Justiça Federal
Colombia_Justification:
Colombia broadly and effectively guarantees the right of individuals and communities to file collective actions and constitutional remedies to defend the environment and the rights affected by mining activity.

The 1991 Political Constitution enshrines in Articles 79 and 80 the right of all persons to enjoy a healthy environment and the duty of the State to protect the diversity and integrity of the environment. Furthermore, Article 88 recognizes class action lawsuits and public interest lawsuits as legal mechanisms for the protection of collective rights and interests, including environmental ones.

Law 472 of 1998 develops this constitutional mandate, regulating class action lawsuits for the immediate protection of collective rights and interests such as the enjoyment of a healthy environment, the existence of ecological balance, the rational management of natural resources, and the defense of public assets. It also provides for public interest lawsuits (Articles 46 to 67), which allow communities affected by mining activities to obtain collective redress for environmental or social damages.

Additionally, the tutela action (Article 86 of the Constitution) provides an expedited mechanism for protecting fundamental rights—such as the rights to life, health, water, and participation—when these are threatened or violated by mining projects. The compliance action (Law 393 of 1997) allows for demanding the effective enforcement of environmental regulations or mining licenses that the authorities are not implementing.

Peru_Justification: Yes, collective action mechanisms and constitutional remedies exist in Peru that allow communities and citizens to defend themselves against the environmental impacts of mining. The most relevant are the amparo action, the popular action, and the compliance action, in addition to the possibility for communities to act directly to claim fundamental rights such as health, water, and a healthy environment.
